Short Description (New): SJP organized an impromptu protest on campus in "commemoration of 71 years since the Nakba," where expression included the demonization of Israel, denying Jews self-determination and promoting BDS [1].
Description: SJP organized an impromptu protest on campus in "commemoration of 71 years since the Nakba," where expression included the demonization of Israel, denying Jews self-determination and promoting BDS [1]. This included: "Long live the martyrs of the great march of return, Long live Palestinian resistance," "Free Gaza, Free Palestine," "Israel is an apartheid state," "BDS," "How many kids are you going to kill, Bibi?". In addition, a speaker said, "Over 400 villages were ethnically cleansed and we are here in commemoration of the struggle Palestinians have gone through over 7 decades. Free Palestine, long live Palestine... Israel is an apartheid state... Over the course of a year, over two hundred Palestinians were killed for simply protesting, for demonstrating their right for self-determination, and thousands more were permanently disabled in what was called the Great Return march. So in solidarity with them we are here today. Not another nickel not another dime no more money for Israel’s crimes." Link 1

Freeman demonized Israel, accusing it of “systematic destruction of [Palestinian] lives” and called for BDS. Kricorian and Hacker also called for BDS. Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): The Associated Student's Black Women's Health Collaborative Board released a statement in solidarity with SJP and the "Palestinian Liberation Movement" which demonized Israel throughout and condoned terrorism, and included the following statements: "We strongly condemn the genocide of the Palestinian people and recognize the generations of settler colonialism inflicted by Israel;" "To comprehend the situation in Palestine, it is essential to recognize that this is not a civil war but people defending themselves against a fascist government;" "We hold the Israeli Government unequivocally accountable for the present upsurge in violence;" "UCSB Administrations' absence of condemnation for Israel's brutality persistently neglects the safety and well-being of Palestinian students on campus;" "Black Women's Health Collaborative holds firmly with the doctrines of the Black Radical Imagination and Black Feminist Theory to establish the structure and values of our organization and its directives;" and "Free Palestine".
Description: The Associated Student's Black Women's Health Collaborative Board released a statement in solidarity with SJP and the "Palestinian Liberation Movement" which demonized Israel throughout and condoned terrorism, and included the following statements: "We strongly condemn the genocide of the Palestinian people and recognize the generations of settler colonialism inflicted by Israel;" "To comprehend the situation in Palestine, it is essential to recognize that this is not a civil war but people defending themselves against a fascist government;" "We hold the Israeli Government unequivocally accountable for the present upsurge in violence;" "UCSB Administrations' absence of condemnation for Israel's brutality persistently neglects the safety and well-being of Palestinian students on campus;" "Black Women's Health Collaborative holds firmly with the doctrines of the Black Radical Imagination and Black Feminist Theory to establish the structure and values of our organization and its directives;" and "Free Palestine". The full statement read: "Dear UCSB community, The A.S. Black Women's Health Collaborative expresses support and stands in solidarity with Students for Justice in Palestine (SJP) and the Palestinian Liberation Movement. We mourn alongside the families and give condolences to those affected by the ongoing tragedies in Gaza and West Bank. We acknowledge the systemic oppression endured by Palestinians for generations. The attacks in Gaza are not just isolated incidents; they are part of a larger pattern of aggression and discrimination perpetuated by the Israeli Government. We strongly condemn the genocide of the Palestinian people and recognize the generations of settler colonialism inflicted by Israel. To comprehend the situation in Palestine, it is essential to recognize that this is not a civil war but people defending themselves against a fascist government. It is not merely a conflict but a profound liberation movement tirelessly fighting for justice by individuals who have long been subjected to discrimination. This is a stark example of imperialist exploitation with a significant power imbalance. The world has fallen short, consistently failing Palestine in every conceivable dimension. We hold the Israeli Government unequivocally accountable for the present upsurge in violence. Through settler colonialism, Israel has displaced people by invasion of territories under the control of an imperial power. In the current manifestation, settler colonialism has been sustained and enforced in the system of apartheid. By exerting absolute control and dominance, Israel's regime has exploited the deprivation of fundamental rights in Palestine. Depriving access to food, water, electricity, residency, and healthcare. UCSB Administrations' absence of condemnation for Israel's brutality persistently neglects the safety and well-being of Palestinian students on campus. We are disappointed in the University's failure to support and protect those impacted by the continuous injustices taking place. The A.S. Black Women's Health Collaborative holds firmly with the doctrines of the Black Radical Imagination and Black Feminist Theory to establish the structure and values of our organization and its directives. My Palestinian brothers and sisters, we hold your safety and well-being in our highest regard. The shared struggles of Black and Palestinian people have long been deeply interconnected through the experiences of marginalization and global dispossession. As we extend our heartfelt condolences, we ask that you please educate yourselves further. Free Palestine. 2023-2024 UCSB A.S. Black Women's Health Collaborative Board." Link 1 (Accessed: 10/24/23)

Short Description (New): SJP shared [1] a petition [2] titled "USC DIVEST CAMPAIGN, P1: Add Palestine Flag to Formerly Named VKC & Pass Flag Resolution," that contained language demonizing Israel.
Description: SJP shared [1] a petition [2] titled "USC DIVEST CAMPAIGN, P1: Add Palestine Flag to Formerly Named VKC & Pass Flag Resolution," that contained language demonizing Israel. The petition stated in relevant part: "The Movement for Black Lives and the Free Palestine movement are critically linked; as we commit ourselves to 'confront anti-Blackness and systemic racism,' it is vital that we interrogate the ways in which racism is embedded into broader institutions of militarization and police violence internationally. Thus, while we continue the fight for racial justice in this country, and within the University of Southern California , we must address the ways in which this university has failed to advocate for its Palestinian student population... In order for USC to truly deliver on its promise to confront systemic racism on campus, the university must add the Palestinian flag to the formerly named VKC Building... After this step is taken, USC must implement the following policies to ensure that they support the global effort in ending the occupation of Palestine: 1. Eliminate the policy that states a country must be recognized in order to be represented on university buildings... 2. Officially endorse both the 'Black Lives Matter' and 'Free Palestine' movements... There are not only crucial parallels between the institutions that uphold a racist U.S. and a racist Israel— there are direct, dire connections between them. The violence both nations enact is rooted in a prison industrial complex that extracts profit out of mass incarceration, a military industrial complex that transforms war and imperialist violence into lucrative enterprises, and in a founding drive to preserve white supremacist states. Through a system dubbed the 'deadly exchange,' the Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) trains and aids U.S. police forces, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), and Customs and Border Protection (CBP). We remain grateful that organizers from the Movement for Black Lives have repeatedly echoed the call of Angela Davis and other Black feminists to recognize that the liberation of Black Americans and the liberation of Palestinians are bound together. These demands must be met in order for USC President Carol Folt to hold true on her promise 'to confront anti-Blackness and systemic racism, and unite as a diverse, equal, and inclusive university.' The strides made these last few weeks towards racial justice are only the beginning, and it’s imperative for USC to maintain their aforementioned commitment to liberation, both here and abroad." Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): CUNY For Palestine, CUNY Law SJP and the anti-Zionist CUNY Law Jewish Law Students Association [1] held the event, "IHRA and the Co-Optation of the Struggle Against Antisemitism: Intersectional Palestine Organizing at CUNY and Beyond," which was advertised with demonizing language that referred to "Israel's settler-colonial ideology"[2].
Description: CUNY For Palestine, CUNY Law SJP and the anti-Zionist CUNY Law Jewish Law Students Association [1] held the event, "IHRA and the Co-Optation of the Struggle Against Antisemitism: Intersectional Palestine Organizing at CUNY and Beyond," which was advertised with demonizing language that referred to "Israel's settler-colonial ideology"[2]. The advertisement read, "Last month, the New York Post reported that CUNY Chancellor Félix Matos Rodríguez, bowing to pressure from right-wing politicians (including some who masquerade as progressives), announced that CUNY would be adopting the IHRA definition of antisemitism. He did so without consultation of the larger CUNY community, and despite the fact that just a year earlier, the CUNY University Student Senate, the governing body of CUNY students system-wide, resisted similar right-wing pressure and voted to reject this definition. The USS, together with many human rights and civil liberties groups, noted that the definition represses and violates the right to free speech and political expression. More than that, the IHRA definition has a clear anti-Palestinian subtext—a subtext which has animated many overtly violent and racist attacks on our Palestinian students. And, as the Jewish Law Student Association wrote in a recent statement, 'By conflating antisemitism with opposition to Israel’s settler-colonial ideology, not only are Palestinian voices silenced, but Jewish students are painted as a monolith, and the long and deep history of Jewish antizionism and Jewish dissent is erased.' Please join us for this virtual teach-in to hear from Fatima Mohammed and Rachel Pincus, CUNY Law School students who have been active in opposing the campaign of political repression behind the adoption of the IHRA definition, and to learn about opportunities for future organizing and mobilization for intersectionalist struggles at CUNY." Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): Society of Fellows and Heyman Center for the Humanities, CPS, Middle East Institute, MESAAS and the Office of the Divisional Deans in the Faculty of Arts and Sciences held the event "Becoming Palestine: Toward an Archival Imagination of the Future," where two speakers demonized Israel.
Description:
Society of Fellows and Heyman Center for the Humanities, CPS, Middle East Institute, MESAAS and the Office of the Divisional Deans in the Faculty of Arts and Sciences held the event "Becoming Palestine: Toward an Archival Imagination of the Future," where two speakers demonized Israel.

Gil Hochberg (Ransford Professor of Hebrew and Comparative Literature, and Middle East Studies at Columbia University, Chair of MESAS):

  • "I think there are things that are specific to Palestine. Let me mention a few things very quickly. The time frame for a settler colonial project. Palestine is a different case than other cases because the time frame is an extended present. I insist in the book that there is no past, there is an extended present. The Nakba happened, it’s happening, it’s continuing to happen. So what we have is a present. There is no place to talk here about reparation, there is a place to talk about return. And the return I opened the book [with] was as a return that inherently I think has to be speculative, because imagining Palestine before is no longer, even in those territorial divisions that we will accept as a state of Palestine today, right. I mean that’s sort of a bigger idea, but there’s an extended present of settler colonialism that has a present and hopefully not a future as these works are suggesting, because there is a particular power to Zionism, it’s a political modality… if we’re going to replace this order, we have to learn how to read it."

Gayatri Gopinath (Associate Professor of Social and Cultural Analysis and Director of the Center for the Study of Gender and Sexuality at New York University):

  • "While Gil’s thinking about the temporality of the archive is rooted in the specificity of Palestine, Israeli settler colonialism, and the Nakba, her insights have implications beyond the region and allow us to see other archival and nationalist projects [and how a] new temporal twist was meant to commemorate the cataclysmic birth of another nation."

Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): The Pembroke Center for Teaching and Research on Women at Brown University signed the statement "Gender Studies Departments in Solidarity with Palestinian Feminist Collective" that demonized Israel and denied Jews self-determination [1].
Description: The Pembroke Center for Teaching and Research on Women at Brown University signed the statement "Gender Studies Departments in Solidarity with Palestinian Feminist Collective" that demonized Israel and denied Jews self-determination [1]. The statement read, "We stand in solidarity with the people of Palestine. We unequivocally answer and amplify the call from the Palestinian Feminist Collective for 'feminists everywhere to speak up, organize, and join the struggle for Palestinian liberation.'... We do not subscribe to a 'both sides' rhetoric that erases the military, economic, media, and global power that Israel has over Palestine. This is not a 'conflict' that is too 'controversial and complex' to assess. Israel is using violent force, punitive bureaucracy, and the legal system to expel Palestinians from their rightful homes and to remove Palestinian people from their land. Israeli law systematically discriminates against Palestinian citizens of Israel. Illegal Israeli settlements choke and police Palestinian communities, and Palestinians are cut off from each other by a network of checkpoints, laws, settler-only highways, and a separation wall that swallows illegally occupied Palestinian land. Both Human Rights Watch and B’Tselem have concluded that Israeli policies and practices towards Palestinians amount to apartheid. As gender studies departments in the United States, we are the proud benefactors of decades of feminist anti-racist, and anti-colonial activism that informs the foundation of our interdiscipline... [We] know that 'Palestine is a Feminist Issue.' In solidarity, we call for the end of Israel's military occupation of Palestine and for the Palestinian right to return to their homes. As residents, educators, and feminists who are also against the settler colonialism of the U.S., we refuse to normalize or accept the United States’ financial, military, diplomatic and political role in Palestinian dispossession. Furthermore, we will not tolerate any censorship of nor retribution against Palestinian scholars, activists, and those openly critical of the Israeli state. We join a vibrant, vast, and growing international solidarity community, composed of those raising their voices in support of Palestinian's right to freedom, return, safety, flourishing, and self-determination. May 15th marked the 73rd anniversary of the Nakba, an ongoing ethnic cleansing of Palestine... Palestinians have been resisting settler colonialism for more than one hundred years. We hail the fortitude and determination of the Palestinian people, who remain, despite the fragmentation of their populations, united in their demands to end their oppression." Link 1

Short Description (New): School of International and Public Affairs (SIPA) held the event "The One State Reality: What is Israel/Palestine," where panelists engaged in demonizing rhetoric of Israel [1].
Description:
School of International and Public Affairs (SIPA) held the event "The One State Reality: What is Israel/Palestine," where panelists engaged in demonizing rhetoric of Israel [1].

Michael Barnett (Author, University Professor of International Affairs and Political Science, George Washington University):

  • "This is where we're at, and the consequence then is that as the apartheid concept becomes more and more acceptable, it is in Israel in many ways and among Jewish Americans, that's going to lead to a real clash. You can't have the values that Jewish Americans want to explain their own and yet support a state that systematically engages in policies and practices that are designed to institutionalize Jewish Supremacy."
  • "[A]t the end of the day to actually even talk about Palestinian rights has always been that sort of nuclear bomb because it would essentially mean the end of a Jewish state. It would require... a greater form of equality that would erase the fundamental fact that this is a Jewish state."

Marc Lynch (Author, Professor of Political Science and International Affairs; Director, Project on Middle East Political Science; Director, M.A. Middle East Studies, George Washington University):

  • "[I]f you are a Palestinian citizen of Israel that gives you one set of rights, which are inferior to those non-Jewish citizens of Israel. If you are Palestinian non-citizen of Israel you have far fewer rights, if any. And if you live in Gaza you have basically no rights at all other than to be... surrounded, starved, bombarded, and basically deprived [of all] human rights whatsoever."
  • "[T]o me one of the most interesting things about all of this is just the sheer rapidity with which you've seen the move of things like one state reality and akin to apartheid enter into the mainstream of discourse... Certainly there was a very vibrant and robust Palestinian discourse about apartheid about the one state reality but it tended to stay within the confines of that one sphere."

Shibley Telhami (Author, Anwar Sadat Professor for Peace and Development, University of Maryland):

  • "When you shift and put on the pair of glasses and you look at the entire state as one, and then you know you can't avoid appealing to the label of apartheid when you describe their relationship between that state and... the five million Palestinians in the West Bank and Gaza."

Link 1 (Accessed: 8/3/23)

Short Description (New): A student published an op-ed in the student newspaper Swarthmore Phoenix titled "The Board is Not Our Friend" that demonized Israel and advocated for BDS.
Description: A student published an op-ed in the student newspaper Swarthmore Phoenix titled "The Board is Not Our Friend" that demonized Israel and advocated for BDS. The op-ed stated, "In the spring of my freshman year, I met with two Board members and a variety of administrators about a resolution that garnered over one thousand signatures and ultimately passed our Student Government Organization. The resolution called upon Swarthmore to divest from seven companies that have documented links to Israeli apartheid and war crimes targeting Palestinians... As long as we remain invested in longstanding state violence against Palestinians, our college is in direct contradiction to its professed values. Unfortunately, however, divesting from apartheid and war crimes is not so simple for Swarthmore. Upon meeting with these Board Members to advocate for our popular Boycott Divestment and Sanctions resolution, we were met with swift rejection... Apartheid is not complicated. It is a violent and racist system that is antithetical to our community’s shared values. Apartheid was not complicated in South Africa and it is not complicated in Palestine. Human rights groups like B’Tselem and Human Rights Watch have recently stated what Palestinians have long known: Israel is committing crimes of apartheid. So why do our Board members seem to think funding apartheid is so complicated? Well, simply put, it is because the Board is not our friend... Don’t get me wrong, Swarthmore’s adoption of our just and fairly moderate divestment policy targeting seven companies explicitly involved in war crimes will not end Palestinian suffering by any means. But it could very well start a chain reaction, much like the cultural and economic boycott of South Africa did and like the fossil fuel divestment campaign originating at Swarthmore continues to... So what do we do? We can start by individually adopting Palestinian civil society’s call to boycott Israeli products until its government complies with International Law. Furthermore, alumni and graduating seniors of conscience can redirect intended donations to Swarthmore toward Palestinians resisting apartheid as encouraged by Swarthmore Jewish Voice for Peace (JVP). Current students should also get involved with the Ban the Ban coalition to start changing the course of our horrendous institutional legacy. Now is the time." Link 1

Description:
The Media Education Foundation and UMass Resistance Studies Initiative held the event “Criminalizing Dissent – The Attack on BDS & American Democracy” [1] with Roger Waters (recorded video message), Omar Barghouti, Cornell West, Dima Khalidi, Shaun King, Tim Wise, Sut Jhally and Linda Sarsour, where speakers espoused rhetoric that denied Jews self-determination, demonized Israel and supported BDS [2-7].

Narrator in Introductory Video Shown [2]:

  • "BDS calls for boycotting Israel until it complies with international law by ending its illegal military occupation of Palestinian land, recognizing the full equality of Palestinian citizens of Israel, and assuring the right of Palestinian refugees to return to their homes."
  • "Since its founding the BDS movement has inspired people around the world to take action against Israel and to stand alongside the Palestinian people in solidarity, It's become especially active on American college campuses."

Roger Waters [3]:

  • "So, here we are November the 12th already. You are all gathered there at UMass Amherst for another pro-BDS meeting... I applaud you for doing it again. I'm sure it'll be instructive and informative and a great success."
  • "The BDS movement is growing and it's growing more powerful and it's beginning to frighten those who support the racist apartheid government of Israel in its settler colonialist occupation of Palestinian lands in the West Bank and its siege of Gaza. So all BDS is trying to do is to encourage the idea that equal human rights are necessary, particularly in the wake of 1948, in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights by the United Nations for all peoples irrespective of their ethnicity or religion or nationality. So that means that the indigenous people of Palestine who are not Jewish should have the same basic civil and human rights as either indigenous people, or all people who live now in Palestine who are Jewish... it's very, very cut and dried and simple."
  • "So I'm with you in spirit, my heart is with you, have a great night, that's all I have to say. BDS [puts up the powerfist]."

Omar Barghouti [4]:

  • "Israel's far-right regime is not merely continuing its decades-old military occupation, settler colonialism, apartheid and ethnic cleansing against Palestinians. It's increasingly outsourcing its anti-democratic tactics to the US."
  • "The BDS movement for Palestinian rights has become a significant partner in a growing international progressive way fighting for global justice against the forces of fascism, xenophobia and savage neoliberalism. Gaza is under attack again today. But even before the horror of Israel's full-blown bombing from the start of the Great March of Return in 2018 to end Israel's brutal siege and assert the right of return for Palestinian refugees, Israeli army snipers have implemented what Human Rights Watch calls a premeditated shoot to kill or maim policy slaughtering dozens and injuring thousands of peaceful Palestinians including children medics, journalists and with disabilities."
  • "Israel's land grabbing, construction of illegal settlements, and its gradual ethnic cleansing, especially in occupied Jerusalem, the Jordan Valley and the Negev, are accelerating its relentless home demolitions arrests and torture of children, and the apartheid walls are designed to make our lives so bitter..."
  • "Solidarity levels the playing fields with the colonizers and gives us hope, which is resilient and contagious. This is precisely why Israel is desperately trying to suppress the nonviolent BDS movement for Palestinian rights as a key part of Palestinian popular nonviolent resistance and the most effective and helpful form of international solidarity with our struggle for emancipation."
  • "BDS calls for three rights: ending Israel's 1967 occupation, ending its institutionalized system of racial segregation and discrimination, which meets the UN definition of apartheid, and upholding the right of Palestinian refugees to return to their homeland. BDS is deeply rooted in decades of Palestinian popular resistance to settler colonialism."
  • "BDS targets complicity not identity. Since there is nothing 'ish' about Israel's crimes against Palestinians, there's nothing inherently anti-Jewish then [about] a non-violent, morally consistent, human rights struggle to end this system of oppression."
  • "Through intimidation, spying and weaponizing claims of anti-Semitism they're trying to keep Israel... above accountability and beyond censure."
  • "Israel’s far right regime is evidently in no position to accuse anyone of anti-Semitism. Israel's fraudulent. And the anti-Semitism smears against human rights defenders in the anti-racist BDS movement sound as credible as Trump’s environmental protection credentials [audience laughter]."

Cornell West [5]:

  • "We're gonna fight the establishment that will tell the lies about what has been going on since 1948 and what has been going on since 1964? What are you so afraid of the truth? Why don't you don't want us to come to terms with what it is to be a settler colonial regime?”
  • "You don’t have to be so scared, you don’t have to be so intimidated. Straighten your back up and take a stand in the name of integrity, honesty, decency, courage... what kind of human being [will you become]?... Did you take a stand on behalf of those [Palestinian] people whose precious babies and men and women are being assaulted, attacked, whose homes are being bulldozed, whose land is being taken?"

King [6]:

  • "I'm honored to be here, [I'm] honored to be able to support the BDS movement."

Tim Wise [7]:

  • "As a Jew I implore you to not only support BDS but at the very least, to support the debate around this."

Short Description (New): The student newspaper, Michigan Daily, published an op-ed by Students Allied for Freedom and Equality (SAFE), that demonized Israel and denied Jews self-determination [1].
Description: The student newspaper, Michigan Daily, published an op-ed by Students Allied for Freedom and Equality (SAFE), that demonized Israel and denied Jews self-determination [1]. The op-ed stated, "On June 9, President Schlissel sent out a campus-wide email titled 'Supporting one another during the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.' First, the occupation of Palestine is not a 'conflict.' This term implies that two similarly powerful entities are involved in some sort of dispute. Palestine does not have a military or billions of dollars in military funding from the United States. Palestinians in the West Bank live each and every day under brutal military occupation with little to no rights. Palestinians living in the occupied territories are being attacked and killed by settlers on the streets. Palestinians in Gaza have been living under a suffocating siege for over 14 years. Even Palestinians living in the diaspora suffer from Israel’s apartheid regime as they are denied the right of return. When you decide to issue statements that recognize suffering, make sure you are able to distinguish the perpetrator from the victim... let us be clear in stating that it is intellectually false to conflate anti-Zionism with anti-Semitism as some members of the U-M community have done. This course of action on your part indicates the lack of importance and representation that Palestinians have on this campus. It also gaslights Palestinians and their allies by falsely claiming they are perpetuating hateful speech and anti-Semitism when the rhetoric found on the rock was solely criticizing Israel as an apartheid state with documented human rights violations. University Regent Jordan Acker also seems to imply the messaging deliberately displaces the pride flag, which is an example of pinkwashing — referring to when a country, government or organization 'appeals to LGBTQ+ rights in order to deflect attention from its harmful practices.' As administrators, you have done nothing but prove that you do not care to understand the experiences of your Palestinian community members. You are only willing to take effective action when it comes to Zionism being rightfully challenged on campus, but will not do the same when it comes to your Palestinian students who have been victims of hatred and bigotry for years." Link 1

Short Description (New): The Stanford Daily re-published as an op-ed [1] the statement "Stanford Community Protests Israeli State Violence Against Palestinians," [2] that was signed by faculty members and students and which demonized Israel, denied Jews self-determination, called for BDS and claimed, "The carnage we see today cannot be excused through any notion that Israel has a right to 'protect itself'".
Description: The Stanford Daily re-published as an op-ed [1] the statement "Stanford Community Protests Israeli State Violence Against Palestinians," [2] that was signed by faculty members and students and which demonized Israel, denied Jews self-determination, called for BDS and claimed, "The carnage we see today cannot be excused through any notion that Israel has a right to 'protect itself'". The op-ed/statement expressed, "We, the undersigned members of the Stanford community, condemn in the strongest terms possible the state of Israel’s systematic destruction of Palestinian property, homes, and lives. the violence we see unfolding—is but one part of an unbroken history stretching back to the 1948 massacre of over 100 Palestinian men, women, and children in the village of Deir Yassin. Then, as now, Zionists reached beyond UN-mandated borders to expel and kill Palestinians and illegally seize their land... The 2018 'New Basic Law of Israel' makes three fundamental claims: 1) The land of Israel is the historical homeland of the Jewish people, in which the State of Israel was established. 2) The State of Israel is the national home of the Jewish people, in which it fulfills its natural, cultural, religious and historical right to self-determination. 3) The right to exercise national self-determination in the State of Israel is unique to the Jewish people. With terrible clarity then, Israel has declared itself an ethno-nation and converted acts illegal under international law (such as annexing Palestine land, undertaking the collective punishment of Palestinians, abrogating its responsibilities as an occupying power, denying Palestinians in exile their human right to return, et cetera) into legitimate (in solely its own view) manifestations of its 'self-determination.' We in the United States are all too familiar with how a similar evocation of 'Manifest Destiny' legitimated genocide against Indigenous peoples... 'Equality' cannot possibly exist in an ethno-state whose very existence has been declared an apartheid state by Human Rights Watch... The carnage we see today cannot be excused through any notion that Israel has a right to 'protect itself.' Its violent and often deadly expulsions of Palestinians from their homes, its armed attack on ordinary Palestinians and others worshipping during Ramadan, these actions logically extend to Palestinians the right to defend themselves first... We reject any attempt to portray this as the fault of the Palestinian people, and deplore the slaughter of innocent men, women, and children, and the crippling of an already weak Palestinian infrastructure. We urge everyone to refuse to be complicit with Israeli 'business as usual,' and to consider endorsing the Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions movement, begun by over 170 civil organizations in Palestine... BDS, and more generally protests against Israel’s persistent violation of international law and international human rights, is not the purview solely of 'radicals' or leftists... those fighting for Palestinian rights now include groups like the Movement for Black Lives, and labor unions have refused to unload and transport goods from the Occupied Territories. The analogy between anti-Black racism and the persecution of the Palestinians has been the subject of much commentary. We join all those working for basic human rights and the respect of international law in condemning Israel for its brutality and violence, and pledge solidarity with the Palestinian people." Link 1, Link 2
Perpetrator(s): Student group(s), Individual student(s), Individual faculty member(s)

Description: The MITWs (Minority, Indigenous and Third World Studies) Research Group (a research group that includes faculty, graduate students, and undergraduate students in the humanities and the social sciences from various departments in the College of Arts and Science) held a screening of Bruce Robbins's film "Some of My Best Friends are Zionists" followed by a panel discussion with Bruce Robbins (Columbia), Donna Nevel (PARCEO - Participatory Action Research Center) and Russell Rickford (Cornell). The film itself consists of interview snippets from a number of individuals, and includes participants supporting “object[ing] to the basis of the founding of the state of Israel” and the “right of return” which would effectively eliminate Israel as a Jewish state, making delegitimizing comparisons that Israel is “similar in some very fundamental way at least to South African apartheid,” expressing that Israel's security barrier is an "exercise in absolute cruelty" and providing a "Boycott Israeli Apartheid" image on a water bottle to show support for the “apartheid analogy.” Following the film, panelist Bruce Robbins called for the right of return, which he said "must be recognized.” Panelist Donna Nevel called for the right of return and for BDS, stating that “for those of us who feel supporting BDS is important as part of a platform… In my view… understanding the Nakba and really deeply having that understanding [then] the right of return is a logical extension of that.” Panelist Russel Rickford denied Jews self-determination, stating, "I don’t believe that there should be such a thing as a Jewish state." Rickford also alluded to Israel as the "Jewish Empire" and falsely accused Israel of "ethnic cleansing." Link 1, Link 2, Link 3, Link 4, Link 5, Link 6, Link 7

Short Description (New): Harvard College Palestinian Alliance, SJP Marquette, Jewish Coalition for Peace, Progressive Asian American Advocacy at Harvard College (TAPAS), Society of Arab Students, Harvard Islamic Society, and Act on a Dream held the event, “Refugee Right of Return” [1] as part of “Palestine Awareness Week 2020,” where both the invited speaker and moderator engaged in language that demonized Israel, and the invited speaker promoted the imposition of sanctions on Israel and the Palestinian right of return, stating that "we would have to dismantle the colonial apartheid regime of Israel" in order for that to occur [2].
Description:
Harvard College Palestinian Alliance, SJP Marquette, Jewish Coalition for Peace, Progressive Asian American Advocacy at Harvard College (TAPAS), Society of Arab Students, Harvard Islamic Society, and Act on a Dream held the event, “Refugee Right of Return” [1] as part of “Palestine Awareness Week 2020,” where both the invited speaker and moderator engaged in language that demonized Israel, and the invited speaker promoted the imposition of sanctions on Israel and the Palestinian right of return, stating that "we would have to dismantle the colonial apartheid regime of Israel" in order for that to occur [2].

Lubnah Shomali (BADIL):

  • “Who are Palestinian refugees? Palestinian refugees are any persons who have been displaced outside the area of what became Israel and the occupied territory of 1967… approximately 750,000 Palestinians were displaced outside what came to be called Israel and entered into the territory that became the West Bank the Gaza strip and then also outside of mandatory Palestine. A significant amount were also displaced internally inside what came to be called Israel, about 40 to 60,000 from 1948 onward. And then in 1967, when Israel occupied the rest of Palestine, another half a million Palestinians were displaced - some of them for the second time - becoming either refugees again or becoming internally displaced persons. The total number of Palestinian refugees and internally displaced persons today numbers approximately 8.7 million, that's approximately 66% of the total worldwide population of Palestinians.”
  • “The ongoing Nakba is characterized by two main factors: the ongoing displacement and dispossession of the Palestinian people, coupled with keeping them away, or in other words, denying their right of return.”
  • “It's a continuous and ongoing crime that Israel commits in both the continuous displacement and the denial of return. When we talk about reparations, it actually means a repairing or a return to what once was. And obviously we won't be able to go back to a situation that existed exactly the way it did prior to the creation of Israel. There are certain things that we have to take into consideration… namely Jewish Israelis who have been residing in what is mandatory Palestine - the homeland for Palestinians - for quite some time and they have acquired certain rights. And so, these things have to be taken into consideration as well. What reparations does is that it places everyone on an equal footing under the law and so the colonizer is no longer a colonizer, the refugee is no longer a refugee, but rather a returnee, and both of these individuals have entitlements and rights.”
  • [In response to the question by the moderator, “What steps uh would have to be taken to ensure that a return of Palestinian refugees to 1948 lands would not lead to a ghettoization of Palestinians within Israel?]: “We would have to dismantle the colonial apartheid regime of Israel.”
  • “It's inevitable because all colonial apartheid regimes must come to an end and so return is inevitable for Palestinians, just as it is for any other displaced population in the world.”
  • “The question that comes up is what are the rights of Israeli Jews under this framework of return? So let's say, for example, if a Palestinian were to return to a property that is now occupied by an Israeli Jew… what are the rights of the current occupants of the property under a new framework? International law is very explicit on this issue. The priority is giving to the returnee, which would be the Palestinian refugee or Internally Displaced Persons to claim that property from the occupier or the former colonizer… The Israeli talking point that Jews would be driven into the sea, this is not what international law allows for. Any Israeli Jew who wishes to stay in Palestine and as I said live at peace with their neighbors and reject that colonial apartheid aspect of the Israeli state can do so and live in peace with their neighbor, [and] other alternative housing and alternative locations for these [Jewish] individuals has to be found because as I said international law clearly stipulates that if the refugee wants to claim their original property and it still stands, then that family or individual or group has the right to do so. It also says that for commercial property… that property should go to that refugee regardless of the fact of whether it's a commercial property or a private business property or whatever.”
  • “This is why international law has both that positive and negative duty of states to interfere and force the relinquishing of the privilege [of the occupier]. That's why sanctions are important and absolutely necessary if we're going to bring Israel into line with international law. That's when the true peace treaty comes. There has to be a situation of not only ensuring that Palestinians get reparations, but also ensure that those individuals and the entities that were responsible for the human rights violations and crimes are held criminally accountable. And so, we also have to have a measure of justice applied in that situation.”
  • “In the South African situation as well, there was both armed and non-armed resistance which is of course a sanctioned right for colonial and oppressed peoples - the right to resist by legitimate means, armed and non-armed. The South African situation also had a component of armed resistance. We do not have that component anymore despite what Israel says. And the fact of the matter is, it takes power to overturn power, and it's not just armed power, it's also as I said before, the power of the people those who pressure their governments, those who choose boycott and divestment, those who vote for the right people in power. I mean that's one of the main things to create that political will where Israel will be held accountable.”
  • "International law… serves as a guide and it allows us to advocate with a legal basis for Palestine without… being accused of antisemitism, even though we are being accused of being terrorists or militant or any of those other things that Israel accuses us of."

Moderator:

  • “I think it's very interesting because this is exactly what makes the issue of return for Palestinians so difficult for people who believe in Zionism to grapple with, because it's a rights-based approach that confronts Zionism and confronts the settler colonial system that has been set up. And while other kinds of frameworks of thinking about this conflict have thought about a two-state solution, have thought about it in different ways, the refugee issue has been ignored precisely probably because it confronts the settler colonial narrative. And this is exactly why it's so important to talk about it.”

Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): SJP held the event, "Human Rights in Palestine: A Talk with Ajamu Barak," who ran with Jill Stein as the 2016 Vice-Presidential candidate for the Green Party.
Description: SJP held the event, "Human Rights in Palestine: A Talk with Ajamu Barak," who ran with Jill Stein as the 2016 Vice-Presidential candidate for the Green Party. During the event, Baraka delegitimized Israel, claiming, "for many of us in the black liberation movement...[w]e took a position from the very beginning that what we had in Israel Palestine was in fact a colonial situation. We had a situation where we had a settler colony established." He further delegitimized Israel, stating, "We have this notion of…the Israelis as innocent settler nation with a right to exist, and in fact...Palestine cannot be seen as an isolated situation. It has to be seen as one expression...of the continuation of this pan-European colonial capitalist project...A project that is now being recognized...as the primary enemy of collective humanity." He additionally demonized Israelis, stating,"We are organizing...a counter protest against some Zionist groups on our campus [who] are bringing former or current IDF soldiers to speak...We are trying to organize large amounts of people to show support against…the importation of colonizers and human rights abusers on our campuses." He demonized Israel further, stating, "any mortal rational person would end up being opposed to the Israeli state, opposed to Israeli domination." Finally, he expressed support for BDS, saying, "One of the most marvelous tools that have been produced over the last decade or so related to the struggle for Palestinian rights is the BDS movement." An audience member further delegitimized Israel by accusing it of "pinkwashing," a demonizing term implying that Israel is malevolently representing itself as a safe haven for LGBQT people in order to hide Israel's "crimes." Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): Penn Students Against the Occupation of Palestine released a statement to support a sit-in to "save the University City Townhomes," where the statement also demonized Israel [1].
Description: Penn Students Against the Occupation of Palestine released a statement to support a sit-in to "save the University City Townhomes," where the statement also demonized Israel [1]. The statement read, in part, "Penn Students Against the Occupation of Palestine stands in solidarity with our neighbors from Drexel's Community for Justice in their sit-in to save the University City Townhomes...We firmly support the students leading the sit-in at the Main Building. Their refusal to move is an act of solidarity with the struggles of all colonized people, Black Philadelphians and Palestinians alike... Our neighbors in the Townhomes' lives are at stake. As the imperialist encroachment on our Black neighbors becomes more palpable, we are aware of the ways that housing privatization in the US connects to deepening Israeli violence towards Palestinians, most recently in Nablus and the West Bank. We condemn any and all acts of settler colonialism, past or present, and we recognize our responsibility towards building a just future." Link 1 (Accessed: 3/8/23)

Description:
Rutgers NB SJP and Endowment Justice Collective held the event "The Fight for Divestment at Rutgers University - Panel Discussion on Divestment from Israeli Apartheid at the University Level"[1] with speakers Iman Abid, Dov Baum, Doura Erekat and Wassim Kanaan, and moderator Abire Sabbagh, whose advertisement stated, "Join us on November 18th for a panel discussion on the importance of divesting from Israeli apartheid at the University level, and our campaign for Rutgers to divest its current endowment investments in apartheid, sweatshop labor, the military and prison industrial complexes, and more". According to an article in Algemeiner, panelists made demonizing remarks as well as expressed support for BDS [2].

Wassim Kanaan (Vice-Chair for the New Jersey Chapter of American Muslims for Palestine):
  • “[Can Sabbagh] walk us [the audience] through how Israel perpetuates apartheid, and why it is important to name it as an apartheid and settler colonial state?"
  • "Israel is a European outpost in the Middle East, Israel is a white supremacist effort in its inception.”
David Litwin (Co-Founder of Jews for Palestinian Right of Return):
  • "What the BDS movement has shown is that the problem is the presence of a racist, settler colonial, Zionist regime … Someday soon, we’re gonna see the toppling of the apartheid regime."
Iman Abid (Director of Advocacy and Organizing at the US Campaign for Palestinian Rights):
  • "[T]he more that we use language like white supremacy when we talk about Zionism, is really critical."

Short Description (New): Michigan State University Students United for Palestinian Rights (SUPR), Wayne State University SJP and Oakland University SJP held the event “Israeli Militarization and the US Deadly Exchange,” where demonizing statements of Israel were made by the student organization representatives who sponsored the event.
Description:
Michigan State University Students United for Palestinian Rights (SUPR), Wayne State University SJP and Oakland University SJP held the event “Israeli Militarization and the US Deadly Exchange,” where demonizing statements of Israel were made by the student organization representatives who sponsored the event.

MSU SUPR:

  • "[B]oth the United States and Israel share the fact that they are colonial states which means that inherently they have a Native population which they have an interest in suppressing, in order to continue… their existence, it's very important for them to suppress Native uprising and land reclamation and oftentimes the tool for that suppression is, in part, policing and security in general."
  • "Israel constantly brands itself in the international community as a leading expert in counter-terrorism and this so-called experience comes from over seventy years of work displacing more than 750,000 people and maintaining an illegal occupation in order to support a racist hierarchy in the region."
  • "The high-level training [of police officers, law enforcement officials, chiefs, assistant chiefs, ICE agents, U.S. Air Marshals, criminal investigators, Homeland Security, Customs and Border Patrol agents, and FBI agents] promotes the spread of tactics from the top down and reinforces this message of racial profiling, escalation, and excessive force that's all commonplace within the tactics of the IDF and also, police here in the U.S."
  • "The United States has the highest rate of incarceration in the entire world and Israel has the fifth… We have to keep in mind that in both cases (Israel and the United States) the groups targeted are not representative of the national demographics. In America, we know that Black and Brown communities are disproportionately targeted, whereas in Israel, Palestinians and immigrant populations, particularly from Africa, are targeted, such as Ethiopian Jews. And this goes to show that both nations are linked by an interest not only to incarcerate, but also to criminalize people based specifically on their identity and to limit their ability to reintegrate and organize for their own liberation."
  • "All of this is to say that the exchange of police and brutal tactics, and their usage in each respective country, together justifies each country's crimes against humanity. Because as America locks children in cages, Israel also locks children in cages. So, it creates international blinders when thinking about crimes against populations in colonial states."

WSU SJP:

  • "So, going into the Israeli militarization in Gaza, to give you guys just a brief explanation, Israel is a country based off of religion. So, anyone who doesn't have Jewish descent in them they usually don't want anyone living within - like, if you guys look at this map, anything within that yellowish color they want just for Israelis to be in, no Palestinians. So, the militarization in Gaza has, and it continues to be, one of the most unnoticed inhumane acts around the world. It goes very unnoticed and not many people talk about it. And although Gaza is claimed under the Palestinian territory, Israeli militarization continues to strategize ways to take over this piece of land. So, things that the Israeli military does is: all the borders around Gaza are all closed up, so, people in Gaza have no access to the West Bank, access to land ‘48, which people call Israel, access to Egypt, and access to air and maritime space."
  • "When we talk about air that means that the Israeli military controls anything that flies over Gaza and when we talk about maritime space there's a certain amount of miles that a Gaza person, like, a Falastini living in Gaza is able to, take their boat out and if they get too close to the imaginary mile where they're not allowed to pass, the Israeli navy will shoot at them, arrest them, and do any inhumane act that they want."
  • "So, what does the Israeli military do in Gaza? For one, unlawful killings. They kill women, they kill children, they kill the disabled, they kill paramedics, and they kill journalists."

OU SUP:

  • "Sometimes these abductions don't always end in the person being imprisoned, without any death. Those two names [previously recited] are just two of countless names- two of countless, that were actually killed in these night raids. They weren't imprisoned, they were straight up executed on-site, in their own homes, in the middle of the night."

Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): The Daily UW published an article titled "There are no 'two sides' to genocide" that demonized Israel, as well as chastised the University for opposing BDS [1] and offering courses on Israel.
Description: The Daily UW published an article titled "There are no 'two sides' to genocide" that demonized Israel, as well as chastised the University for opposing BDS [1] and offering courses on Israel. The article stated, "Institutions like the UW have reinforced this two-side narrative... But presenting this situation as a 'conflict' or a 'two-sided' story goes beyond naïve and becomes grotesque... On one hand are the Palestinian people, who lost their land to British imperialism and resisted for 73 years with little or no advanced technology. They used obsolete rockets and homemade weapons to fight back against the military attacks, oppression, and segregation to which a declared Zionist state subjected them. On the other hand is a state and technological power with a straightforward program of ethnic cleansing, supported by the leading imperialist powers, especially the United States... This collage of facts fails to mention that since 1948, Israel colonized Palestine and expelled more than 750,000 Palestinians from their land and murdered thousands of people (including more than 2,000 children)... Israel denied vaccines to the Palestinian population... The UW has also endorsed this two-sided narrative in the past. In 2014, the UW opposed the boycott of universities and faculties in Israel. The UW also has various exchange programs through the Jackson School of International Studies in which students are invited to know more about Israeli history... [According to a SUPER UW member], 'Recently Ana Mari Cauce, in an email response [to a SUPER member], talked about how UW’s Israel studies program is one of the most prestigious in the country and how the university cares about academic freedom and providing diverse perspectives and viewpoints. [It's] as if viewpoints that propagate genocide are justifiable to be included in their curriculum or within an educational institution... like, what are you teaching people?' Israel’s oppression of Palestine reveals its colonialist and imperialist practices, which do not deserve any kind of support from nations or institutions that have a minimal commitment to democracy... Everybody has to denounce what is happening in Palestine. There is no conflict or fight; it is colonialism and ethnic cleansing in its most visceral form." Link 1

Classification: DENYING JEWS SELF-DETERMINATION, DEMONIZATION, CALLS FOR BDS
Short Description (New): SJP, MECHA De ASU and YDSA at ASU issued a "Joint Statement on USG Senate Session" which demonized Israel throughout, denied Jewish claims of antisemitism as "baseless claims", denied Jews self-determination and called for BDS. [1]
Description: SJP, MECHA De ASU and YDSA at ASU issued a "Joint Statement on USG Senate Session" which demonized Israel throughout, called Jewish claims of antisemitism "baseless," denied Jews self-determination, and called for BDS. [1] The statement read: Last night, November 14th, 2023, students organized to make their voices heard at the Undergraduate Student Government Tempe Senate Session, initially planning to show support for a bill endorsing Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions (BDS) on the State of Israel. However, due to reasons that will be outlined below, this bill was not heard last night. We still showed up, with an estimated 120 people coming out in support of Palestine... As you all may know, we planned to introduce our BDS bill at the Senate meeting tonight. However, the bill was blocked by the USG executives due to ‘procedural’ and ‘jurisdictional’ reasons that, in our opinion, are all excuses to prevent the bill from being heard. SJP has been attempting to pass a BDS resolution for years, with this last attempt being the third in the last 5 years. The first of the three, introduced in 2019, was taken off the agenda last minute and not heard by the Senate. The second, introduced in 2021, was taken off the agenda by the Senate President for similar reasons that this current one was, despite significant support from Senators and the community. The procedural issue came about because the executives, specifically the Student Body President Morgann Kelly, claimed that we did not submit the bill on time. We submitted the bill to be reviewed by the Senate President, William Hanson on Wednesday, November 8th. President Hanson initially promised the bill would be reviewed by the end of day Wednesday. Then, after hearing no response, we asked again and he said it would be done by Thursday night or Friday morning at the latest. Despite these promises, he continued to ignore us, and stalled deciding on the bill until Sunday morning, stating that the bill will not be heard. Student Body President Morgann Kelly stated that the bill needed to be approved and submitted by Friday at 2:30pm, and did not address President Hanson's delay of the approval process. We gave them ample time to approve the bill and they lied to us more than once about approval, and then attempted to claim that it was actually our fault that it wasn't submitted on time. They used their own bureaucratic incompetence to justify killing the bill. In terms of ‘jurisdiction’, Student Body President Kelly claimed that BDS- rhetoric cannot be included in the bill because it could alienate Jewish and Israeli students. Aside from the fact that BDS is a humanitarian response to the illegal occupation of Palestine, BDS is a nonviolent protest movement supported by people of all backgrounds, including anti-Zionist Jewish people. Non-partisan organizations like Jewish Voice for Peace, the National Lawyers Guild, and many more have endorsed BDS, despite the false claims of the movement being antisemitic. President Kelly also stated that it is not within USG's jurisdiction ‘to manage and advise the university’, which begs the question: what is the point of student government if not to represent ASU students to the administration? Just as SJP President Finn Howe asked, if ‘it's not USGs role to advise Arizona State University, when is the University going to listen to its students?’. Why do we even have a Student Government if they aren't allowed to advise the university on what the students actually want? We will now address the very successful protest that we conducted last night. Despite massive waves of misinformation on Twitter, the event was nonviolent and there was no antisemitic rhetoric being spread by any of our supporters. We challenge our opponents to provide one shred of evidence that any antisemitic rhetoric was lodged at any of the Jewish student's speaking in support of Israel last night. They will find none of it. The focus of this demonstration was to draw attention to ASU's investments in occupied Palestine, and how those investments directly contribute to the ethnic cleansing of Palestinians. We also wanted to highlight the university's endorsement of these actions by Israel, when it signed onto a statement in support of Israel [1]. The university is an active participant in America's imperialist project, and by extension, the Israeli colonial project in Palestine. According to Human Rights Watch, there are ‘verified videos taken in Lebanon and Gaza on October 10 and 11, 2023, respectively, showing multiple airbursts of artillery-fired white phosphorus over the Gaza City port and two rural locations along the Israel- Lebanon border,’ which constitutes a violation of international law and a war crime [2]. According to Amnesty International, Israel has carried out ‘attacks [that] violated international humanitarian law, including by failing to take feasible precautions to spare civilians, or by carrying out indiscriminate attacks that failed to distinguish between civilians and military objectives, or by carrying out attacks that may have been directed against civilian objects’ [3]. These are the actions that ASU has endorsed by publicly declaring its support for Israel. We also want to draw attention to antisemitism on campus. Despite baseless claims of BDS and the pro-Palestinian movement being antisemitic, there has been several antisemitic incidents on campus in the last few years. None of them, however, were perpetrated by supporters of Palestine. Far right white supremacists on campus have been harassing Jewish students, creating an environment of fear, and hosting bigoted speakers on campus, all without repercussions from ASU. In 2022, ASU allowed College Republicans United to host Jared Taylor, an antisemitic white supremacist, on campus [4]. This was widely condemned by a large number of student groups, including Students for Justice in Palestine, but the university decided that allowing this antisemite to incite violence on campus falls within the bounds of freedom of speech. In 2020, the same group that hosted Jared Taylor also was caught putting up violently antisemitic posters around ASU dorms [5]. Once again, ASU ‘investigated’ this incident but took no meaningful action in response to it, and this club is still allowed on campus. In fact, according to Jewish students last night, ASU's administration has actually told them that they are better off hiding their Jewishness if they feel afraid. While we personally feel that their fear towards pro-Palestinian protestors is misdirected, it is absolutely ridiculous that the university would actually tell a minority student to ‘hide being Jewish’. This is why we call on ASU's Jewish students to recognize that we are not their enemy, but that the university is the one that allows true antisemitism to thrive. Time and time again, our groups have stood strongly against antisemitism while the university remains silent. Once again, we challenge our detractors to provide evidence of any violence or threats of violence towards Jewish students. Our movement is not antisemitic, and includes many Jewish people who stand with the Palestinians in their struggle for self- determination and liberation from occupation. We also demand an immediate retraction from any public parties that are claiming that there was violence occurring at the event, that there was antisemitic rhetoric, and that Jewish students received death threats. These claims, which have no evidence, amount to defamation in our opinion. We also invite everyone to view the full unedited footage of the event on SJP at ASU's Instagram to see the event as it truly occurred [6]. As far as the alleged rock throwing, we are aware of the claims but would like to emphasize that we are not aware of any person that threw rocks, and none of the rhetoric being used was encouraging people to do so. If the claims are true, the individual acted entirely on their own free will and is not linked to any of the organizations that put on the event. To conclude, we wanted to once again thank everyone who showed up last night. We showed the university and USG that the ASU student body will not stand idly by as our university both funds and rhetorically supports a genocide. This fight didn't end yesterday... it has only just begun. We invite everyone interested in further advocacy for Palestinian rights to join us in our coalition to stop financial support for Apartheid on campus, which we have named ‘Students Against Apartheid’. The campaign will begin with a boycott event outside of the Memorial Union Starbucks tomorrow, November 16th. More information will be posted on the Students Against Apartheid Instagram, @saga.asu... From the River to the Sea, Palestine Will be Free" Link 1 (Accessed: 12/8/23)

Description: Bates Leftist Coalition [1] and SJP [2] shared an alumni letter, "Bates College Alumni Solidarity Statement," that demonized Israel [3]. The statement read, "Several days ago, members of the Bates College community wrote 'Free Palestine,' 'Stop Ethnic Cleansing,' and 'F*ck Zionist Israel' in chalk on campus buildings. The administration received a complaint that speaking out against Israeli apartheid and settler-colonialism constituted a hate crime and responded by making a report to the Lewiston Police Department. The resulting police investigation was quickly dropped. We, the undersigned alumni of Bates College, stand in firm solidarity with these community members as they voice their support for Palestinian liberation. We unequivocally condemn the decision to involve the police and subject these students to the threat of criminal prosecution... It is not antisemitic to condemn Israel or to support Palestinian freedom. We are inspired by students’ activism on Palestine... Israel’s latest attacks on Palestinians are not, as the Bates administration wrote in an email to students, an intensification in the 'Israeli-Palestinian Conflict.' Israel’s attacks are a continuation of decades-long ethnic cleansing of Palestinians... This violent situation does not create safety for Palestinian or Jewish people. We call on Bates to speak against these atrocities." Link 1, Link 2, Link 3

Short Description (New): The student government discussed a BDS resolution which failed after a 10-hour marathon meeting, with a final vote occurring at 5 am in a secret ballot with 10 yeses, 14 noes and 0 abstentions.
Description:
The student government discussed a BDS resolution which failed after a 10-hour marathon meeting, with a final vote occurring at 5 am in a secret ballot with 10 yeses, 14 noes and 0 abstentions. It was the sixth attempt in seven years to try to pass a BDS resolution [1]. During the meeting, statements denying Jews self-determination and demonizing Israel were made by unidentified students [2] in support of the BDS resolution:

  • [Responding to a Senate member's question about Omar Barghouti having said that when BDS is implemented there will be no Jewish state and all Jews will have to be removed from that area]: "It’s going to completely hamstring this entire discussion if we combine different issues. The issue of ending human rights violations cannot be tied in some inherent way to the issue of wanting Jews out of Palestine. I think on the other hand the question of what does a Jewish state mean needs to be visited. I think the question of whether we want an ethno-state should be visited... I think these are all different questions that need to be [and] should be looked at."
  • "I think one of the best ways to curb the right wing is by not supporting ethnic cleansing [in Israel], not supporting violence, not supporting humanitarian destruction."
  • "I call upon AS Senate to support this resolution and divest from companies that take part in human rights violations in Palestine. I urge the senate to stop allowing this university to be complicit in the massacre and death of Palestinian people by divesting from companies like Northup Grumman... I want to emphasize the reality of the situation. Israel is the mirror of US imperialism. It mirrors the US in that Israel is also a settler colonial project... divesting from these companies would be one permanent step necessary. Please use your privilege as senate to vote in favor of this resolution."

Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): The Carsey-Wolf Center, the Mellichamp Global Studies Initiative, the Center for Middle East Studies, the Department of Film and Media Studies, the Orfalea Center for Global and International Studies, and the Department of the History of Art and Architecture co-sponsored the event, “Social Impact Cinema: Dreaming Palestine – The Time that Remains.
Description: The Carsey-Wolf Center, the Mellichamp Global Studies Initiative, the Center for Middle East Studies, the Department of Film and Media Studies, the Orfalea Center for Global and International Studies, and the Department of the History of Art and Architecture co-sponsored the event, “Social Impact Cinema: Dreaming Palestine – The Time that Remains.” A discussion with Sherene Seikaly, Steven Salaita and moderator Maryam Griffin followed the screening of the film. During the discussion, Seikaly delegitimized Israel, asking “What does a just future mean for Palestinians in this context of now a hundred years of settler colonialism,” implying Israel is a settler-colonial state. Griffin also delegitimized Israel, saying, “What some people might call present-day Israel it occurred to me that there might be some problem with the word occupation so oftentimes when people refer to the occupied territories they're talking about the West Bank they're talking about Gaza, they're referring with that term to the occupation that happened in 1967, but here we really have a story about another occupation, the occupation that happened in 1948.” Link 1, Link 2

Description: SJP released a "SJP Statement on The Israeli Murdering of Palestinian Youth" that demonized Israel, referred to the IDF as the "Israeli Occupation Forces" as well as called to support BDS [1]. The statement read, "As the Israeli Occupation Forces (IOF) increases its deadly attacks on Palestinians in the West Bank and Jerusalem, and especially in cities like Nablus and Jenin and refugee camps like Shuafat, Palestinians everywhere have risen up and resisted the attacks. In response to the massive and popular resistance, Israel has continued to collectively punish the Palestinian people by killing dozens of Palestinians, mass arrests, and assassinations. Israel has also besieged the city of Nablus in response to the rise of the popularity of the newly formed Palestinian resistance group the Lion's Den, which is based in Nablus. The IOF has also called in an airstrike during one of its raids on Nablus - the first time they do this in the West Bank since the second intifada. In Shuafat, Palestinians have been attacked and blockaded within the refugee camp. Meanwhile, the IOF and Israeli settlers storm Al-Aqsa Mosque in Jerusalem on a daily basis. Israeli Occupation Forces have martyred 29 Palestinians this month alone. These attacks exemplify Israel's constant attempts to stifle popular support for resistance among Palestinian communities. Their efforts are failing. The youth of Palestine are following in the footsteps of their ancestors as they resist the Occupation through means of popular struggle. The Palestinian people who suffer the consequences of the Occupation on a daily basis have taken to the streets to resist and protect their communities to pave a path toward liberation. This new wave of resistance has been one with unity across Historic Palestine. From Jenin to Nablus to Jerusalem and throughout '48 territories, youth throughout all of Historic Palestine are leading the resistance and showing solidarity with one another through protests and strikes. Palestinians are finding strength in our collective power to resist the Occupation. Palestinians on the ground are calling upon us to organize and mobilize wherever we may be. The Occupation thrives on complicity and silence, and so we must be LOUD. Participate in local protests, share any information on current events on social media, make sure you're following Boycott Divestment & Sanctions (BDS) principles, push your representatives to sign onto HR 2590 (Palestinian Children and Families Act), and continue to voice your support for the resistance of the Palestinian people!" The statement included images from an off-campus banner drop on Chicago’s Eisenhower expressway I-290 by the "Coalition for Justice in Palestine" with banners that stated "Long Live Palestinian Resistance" and "Boycott Isr*el [Palestinian flag]". Link 1

Short Description (New): SUPER UW posted the organization's "Points of Unity" to Instagram, which were discussed at SUPER UW's summer retreat event earlier in August, where the Points of Unity include commitment to recognizing BDS, the "illegitimacy of Zionism and our duty towards anti-normalization," and that the "only just future is an end to the apartheid and a single Palestine from the river to the sea" [1].
Description:
SUPER UW posted the organization's "Points of Unity" to Instagram, which were discussed at SUPER UW's summer retreat event earlier in August, where the Points of Unity include commitment to recognizing BDS, the "illegitimacy of Zionism and our duty towards anti-normalization," and that the "only just future is an end to the apartheid and a single Palestine from the river to the sea" [1]. The full Points of Unity stated:

"We are committed to recognizing:

  • BDS in its full extent.
  • Palestinians' + other occupied peoples' right to resist by any means necessary.
  • All Palestinians' right to return to their land.
  • The leadership of the Palestinian popular classes.
  • All forms of discrimination work within capitalism against marginalized peoples.
  • The illegitimacy of Zionism and our duty towards anti-normalization.
  • The only just future is an end to the apartheid and a single Palestine from the river to the sea.
  • SUPER operates on colonized land of the Duwamish and Coast Salish peoples, and we will do our utmost to support all Indigenous communities in their fights for justice.
  • Zionism is inextricable from the US' global hegemonic agenda; the fight for Palestinian liberation is internationalist and anti- imperialist at its core."

Image credit: Screenshot SUPER UW / Instagram. Link 1 (Accessed: 8/31/23)

Short Description (New): An article in The Amherst Student titled "Human Rights Leader Speaks on Israeli Treatment of Palestinians" summarized a talk by Omar Shakir and referenced his demonizing claims of Israel engaging in "apartheid" [1].
Description: An article in The Amherst Student titled "Human Rights Leader Speaks on Israeli Treatment of Palestinians" summarized a talk by Omar Shakir and referenced his demonizing claims of Israel engaging in "apartheid" [1]. The article stated, "Shakir’s presentation was a summary of the 214-page report in which HRW defines the Israeli treatment of Palestinians as apartheid. He began by providing a summary of the situation: The Israeli government rules over the area between the Jordan river and the Mediterranean sea, and it systematically privileges Jewish Israelis while repressing Palestinians. His argument was systematic and empirical in nature, which is part of what made the presentation ‘extremely convincing,’ said attendee Hannah Kim ’25. The first step was understanding the on-the-ground reality in Israel through case studies and past data. ‘The question was, ‘How does Israel treat Palestinians?’’ said Shakir. The second step was to apply the legal definition of apartheid to the facts the report identified. Shakir defined the crime of apartheid under international law as ‘inhumane acts committed with the intent to dominate amid a context of systematic oppression of one group over another.’ During his presentation, he applied the case studies and data to each element of this definition: (1) Israel’s intent to dominate, (2) systematic oppression of Palestinians, and (3) inhumane acts carried out against the Palestinians. He first explained Israeli intent to dominate through strategic management of land ownership. In Jerusalem, Israeli policy-planning documents lay out the goal of maintaining a Jewish majority in the city, even including target demographic ratios that would ensure a solid Jewish Israeli majority. In the West Bank, Israel’s policy has been to maximize Jewish Israeli land and isolate Palestinian populations in order to, according to the plans,’make it hard for Palestinians to create territorial contiguity and political unity.’ The second element of apartheid is the systematic oppression of an out group. In this case, Shakir referred to the 15-year closure and blockade of the Gaza Strip, which severely restricts entry and exit to and from the territory. ‘For the first three years of the closure, Israel calculated the average caloric intake they wanted to maintain for the population of Gaza and restricted what food was allowed in to match that target level,’ reported Shakir. He also pointed to the fact that the majority of families in Gaza frequently lack electricity, unlike Jewish Israelis, as further evidence of such systematic oppression... Having shown the systematic oppression of the Palestinian people, Shakir explained, HRW identified five clusters of inhumane acts, the third element of apartheid, being carried out against Palestinians... The data in the report, compiled by HRW and other humanitarian groups such as Amnesty International, Al-Haq, and B’Tselem, allows HRW to conclude that Israeli authorities are guilty of apartheid against Palestinians, said Shakir. Shakir also acknowledged the existence of threats to Israeli security, but he noted that ‘none of these policies are even justified by the Israeli government in the name of security.' Just as... `````added... [H]e highlighted the inequality between the populations. ‘A single system methodically engineered to ensure the domination of one Jewish Israeli at the expense of another Palestinian is not a conflict between two equal parties,’ he said… [An organizer] also felt that the event succeeded in ‘shift[ing] the baseline assumption’ that ‘anti-Zionism is violent toward Jewish people.’... [an organizer’s] vision for the discourse around the topic is ‘a recognition that anti-Zionism is not anti-Semitism, that although Israel is a Jewish state, the actions of its government do not reflect Jewish values.’" Link 1 (Accessed: 3/28/23)
