Short Description (New): The Gender and Sexuality Studies Teaching Faculty at Loyola University Maryland signed the statement "Gender Studies Departments in Solidarity with Palestinian Feminist Collective" that demonized Israel and denied Jews self-determination [1].
Description: The Gender and Sexuality Studies Teaching Faculty at Loyola University Maryland signed the statement "Gender Studies Departments in Solidarity with Palestinian Feminist Collective" that demonized Israel and denied Jews self-determination [1]. The statement read, "We stand in solidarity with the people of Palestine. We unequivocally answer and amplify the call from the Palestinian Feminist Collective for 'feminists everywhere to speak up, organize, and join the struggle for Palestinian liberation.'... We do not subscribe to a 'both sides' rhetoric that erases the military, economic, media, and global power that Israel has over Palestine. This is not a 'conflict' that is too 'controversial and complex' to assess. Israel is using violent force, punitive bureaucracy, and the legal system to expel Palestinians from their rightful homes and to remove Palestinian people from their land. Israeli law systematically discriminates against Palestinian citizens of Israel. Illegal Israeli settlements choke and police Palestinian communities, and Palestinians are cut off from each other by a network of checkpoints, laws, settler-only highways, and a separation wall that swallows illegally occupied Palestinian land. Both Human Rights Watch and B’Tselem have concluded that Israeli policies and practices towards Palestinians amount to apartheid. As gender studies departments in the United States, we are the proud benefactors of decades of feminist anti-racist, and anti-colonial activism that informs the foundation of our interdiscipline... [We] know that 'Palestine is a Feminist Issue.' In solidarity, we call for the end of Israel's military occupation of Palestine and for the Palestinian right to return to their homes. As residents, educators, and feminists who are also against the settler colonialism of the U.S., we refuse to normalize or accept the United States’ financial, military, diplomatic and political role in Palestinian dispossession. Furthermore, we will not tolerate any censorship of nor retribution against Palestinian scholars, activists, and those openly critical of the Israeli state. We join a vibrant, vast, and growing international solidarity community, composed of those raising their voices in support of Palestinian's right to freedom, return, safety, flourishing, and self-determination. May 15th marked the 73rd anniversary of the Nakba, an ongoing ethnic cleansing of Palestine... Palestinians have been resisting settler colonialism for more than one hundred years. We hail the fortitude and determination of the Palestinian people, who remain, despite the fragmentation of their populations, united in their demands to end their oppression." Link 1

Short Description (New): SJP released [1] a petition [2] that demonized Israel and called on students to "Sign Our Petition to End Drew University's Complicity in Apartheid".
Description: SJP released [1] a petition [2] that demonized Israel and called on students to "Sign Our Petition to End Drew University's Complicity in Apartheid". The petition read, "As Drew students, we condemn apartheid and mass injustice anywhere and everywhere, which includes Israeli apartheid and Israel’s inhumane treatment and systemic oppression of Palestinians. Amnesty International’s comprehensive report, Israel’s Apartheid against Palestinians: Cruel System of Domination and Crime against Humanity, illuminates how massive seizures of Palestinian land and property, unlawful killings, forcible transfer, drastic movement restrictions, and the denial of nationality and citizenship to Palestinians are constitutive of a system that amounts to apartheid under international law. As we stand against apartheid, it is imperative to address the ways in which our college campus is complicit with Israeli apartheid, particularly through its stock of General Mills products in dining and vending locations. General Mills is a multinational food corporation based in the United States. It is best known for its popular food brands such as Betty Crocker, Nature Valley, Pillsbury, Häagen Dazs, and the popular cereals Cheerios, Chex, Lucky Charms, Trix, and Cocoa Puffs. General Mills is recognized by the UN Human Rights Council and Boycott Divestment Sanctions (BDS) movement as being complicit in Israel’s illegal occupation of Palestinian land and system of apartheid that oppresses its indigenous inhabitants. General Mills maintains and operates a factory in the Atarot Industrial Zone, an Israeli settlement in annexed East Jerusalem. According to several UN resolutions and international observers, these settlements are illegal under the Fourth Geneva Convention. By conducting business and utilizing land and water resources in settlement areas, General Mills is profiting from the crimes of land theft and military occupation. General Mills products are widely available on Drew University’s campus. Our two main dining facilities are always stocked with General Mills cereals and the C-Store is a reliable spot for Häagen Dazs ice cream and Nature Valley bars. Additionally, Cocoa Puffs products can be found in certain residence hall vending machines. Although the Drew administration says that it does not make dining decisions based on political beliefs, the presence of General Mills on campus insinuates that Drew University is complicit in apartheid and oppression. If Drew remains neutral in situations of injustice, it has chosen the side of oppression. Recently, the administration has also condemned Russia’s neglect of international law and the oppression of Ukrainians. It is imperative to ask: why does this double standard exist? For these reasons, Students for Justice in Palestine at Drew University and student signatories demand that General Mills items be removed from our campus." Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): A letter criticizing Temple University and CNN for their responses to Marc Lamont Hill's statements at a UN meeting concerning Israel was sent to the University and signed by various SJP chapters as well as by 12 students, a 1989 alumnus and two professors.
Description: A letter criticizing Temple University and CNN for their responses to Marc Lamont Hill's statements at a UN meeting concerning Israel was sent to the University and signed by various SJP chapters as well as by 12 students, a 1989 alumnus and two professors. The letter demonized Israel and utilized the same statement Hill did that denies Jews self-determination. The letter stated, "During his speech, Hill criticized Israel's state violence and its ethnic cleansing of Palestinians...In his speech, he called on countries to boycott and divest from companies who contribute and invest in the ongoing Israeli settlements... He concluded his speech... [by asking for a] 'commit[ment] to political action, grass-roots action, local action and international action that will give us what justice requires and that is a free Palestine from the river to the sea.' ... ADL’s complicity in the racist policing of Black communities must be taken into consideration when confronting the fact that they applied immense pressure on CNN to fire Marc Lamont Hill; their insistence on Hill’s removal is nothing short of racist... the Dean of Temple University has said that 'Professor Hill does not represent Temple University, and his views are his own [...] Temple condemns in the strongest possible terms all anti-Semitic, racist or incendiary language, hate speech, calls to violence, and the disparagement of any person.' We cannot pretend that painting Hill as a threat that Temple University must remove is fueled by anything other than racism... We... urge Temple University to avoid repeating CNN’s mistake and to stand by Marc Lamont Hill’s right to speak for justice in Palestine." Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): The Student Life published an op-ed titled "Pomona endowment disclosure must include divestment from Israeli occupation" that demonized Israel, denied Jews self-determination and called for BDS [1].
Description: The Student Life published an op-ed titled "Pomona endowment disclosure must include divestment from Israeli occupation" that demonized Israel, denied Jews self-determination and called for BDS [1]. The op-ed stated, in part, "[I]t behooves us to remember that students organizing for Palestinian liberation across the Claremont Colleges have also been seeking to draw attention to the endowment for years, calling for total disclosure of the Colleges’ investments and total divestment from companies on the Boycott, Divestment, Sanctions list — companies complicit in perpetuating the Israeli occupation of Palestine. BDS, a movement in solidarity with Palestinian liberation, calls for the boycott of, divestment from and sanctions on the Israeli apartheid regime. Its goal is to build international pressure to end the illegal military occupation of Palestine, end discrimination against Palestinians living within the state of Israel and to allow Palestinian refugees the right to return to their homes. At the 5Cs, pro-BDS organizers have worked alongside the goal of fossil fuel divestment, understanding that the struggle against profit-driven destruction of the environment and the struggle for decolonization are not only linked, but frequently one and the same fight... The Palestinian liberation struggle, too, has always fought against Israeli greenwashing and environmental destruction, which goes hand in hand with the militarism that undergirds the occupation of Palestine. The same U.S. defense budget that hands over $3 billion (no relation) yearly in military funding to Israel also funds the largest polluter of any individual organization in the world, the U.S. military. The extraction and use of fossil fuels is historically deeply tied to histories of war and imperialism, meaning that a movement for environmental justice that ignores Palestinian liberation fails to get to the root of the problem: the global legacy of colonial extraction and exploitation. Increasingly, students across the 5Cs have recognized this entwinement, and it is no coincidence that support for divestment from fossil fuels and from Israeli apartheid has grown in tandem. Pitzer College has long seen organizing for fossil fuel divestment, and its faculty and student representatives showed their support for BDS and Palestinian liberation in 2019 in their historic vote to suspend Pitzer’s Haifa University study abroad program, later undemocratically overturned by President Melvin Oliver. Pomona’s student government, too, has been a staunch ally of the Palestinian cause, most recently in the unanimous passage in 2021 of a resolution to divest ASPC’s own funds from the Israeli occupation... Educational institutions across the U.S. have joined the 5Cs in recognizing the need for divestment from the Israeli occupation, furthered by a strengthening international consensus that the occupation of Palestine is not only illegal under international law but also constitutive of an apartheid regime... The recent banners dropped across the Claremont Colleges during Palestine Freedom Week made clear their demand: '5C ADMIN: DIVEST FROM ISRAELI APARTHEID.' Calls for endowment disclosure and divestment from fossil fuels without the simultaneous call for BDS are incomplete, and while there may never be an ethical endowment, it is our obligation as students in the U.S. to support the Palestinian struggle for liberation in material ways. I urge ASPC and the 5C student body not to abandon Palestine, but to maintain consistency and moral clarity on this issue, about which there can be no compromise. Moves for endowment disclosure and divestment must address both fossil fuels and investment in the Israeli occupation, not only because there is clear student support on both issues, but because the environmental and Palestinian struggles must be seen as one and the same – as the struggle against the destruction of war and imperialism." Link 1

Short Description (New): Noura Erekat was the keynote speaker of the panel conference event, “6 Days, 50 Years: 1967 and the Politics of Time,” hosted by the Center for Middle Eastern Studies in conjunction with similar centers at UCLA and UC Santa Barbara.
Description: Noura Erekat was the keynote speaker of the panel conference event, “6 Days, 50 Years: 1967 and the Politics of Time,” hosted by the Center for Middle Eastern Studies in conjunction with similar centers at UCLA and UC Santa Barbara. During the keynote, an image during the presentation delegitimized Israel with a headline accusing Israel of "apartheid." Additionally, during the Q & A of the keynote, a question arose about BDS, to which Erekat answered favorably to BDS as a tactic that "normalizes the politics of delegitimization." Prior to the keynote, Joel Beinin, Smadar Ben Natan and Leena Dallasheh held a panel discussion with Noura Erekat as discussant and moderated by Emily Gottreich. During the Q & A of the panel discussion, Leena Dallasheh denied Jews self-determination, stating, "I will say it right out. Israel has no right to exist as a Jewish state just like America has no right to exist as a white state or as a Protestant state which it was established as." Joel Beinin demonized Israel, stating, "If the question was posed, is Jewish supremacy in Israel/Palestine legitimate? Probably not too many people would say yes." Short Description (New): AMED and the People's Forum held the "open classroom" event titled "Lift the blockade of Cuba and Gaza/Palestine now"[1] as part of AMED's "Teaching Palestine: Pedagogical Praxis and the Indivisibility of Justice" series, where speakers engaged in rhetoric that demonized Israel, including one speaker who claimed, "There's a lot of people the US hangs out with that are not good for humanity - Israel is one of them, and so, what we're talking about [with] solidarity is also knowing who is our enemy"[2].
Description:
AMED and the People's Forum held the "open classroom" event titled "Lift the blockade of Cuba and Gaza/Palestine now"[1] as part of AMED's "Teaching Palestine: Pedagogical Praxis and the Indivisibility of Justice" series, where speakers engaged in rhetoric that demonized Israel, including one speaker who claimed, "There's a lot of people the US hangs out with that are not good for humanity - Israel is one of them, and so, what we're talking about [with] solidarity is also knowing who is our enemy"[2].

Rabab Abdulhadi (Director of Arab and Muslim Ethnicities and Diasporas Studies Program at SFSU):

  • "[R]ecognize the coalitions and how wide spread the support for Palestine and for Cuba is despite the noise pollution that you might hear from Washington or from the Zionist groups - and they speak louder, because they have more resources and they have more money and they have more power and so on, but at the end of the day we do know with the resistance of Gaza and Cuba and everywhere else you cannot defeat people."
  • "[T]he third anniversary is the anniversary of the Israeli Apartheid Wall that was built years ago that locks in Palestinian communities."
  • "[T]his is really important to pressure and build the broadest solidarity to enable [Palestinian] people to actually get rid of occupation and exercise the right to self-determination on their land."
  • "Who's talking about antisemitism? Like why do you every time you talk about Palestine you immediately discuss antisemitism … let's sit down and we have a whole discussion about it and what does it mean and the Zionist history and the history of settler colonialism and so on. And the ways in which Zionism and Israel have been able to mix up the whole question of Jewishness and the multiple experiences of Jewishness with the question of Israel in order to mix up things and make it only possible to see Israel as the only solution to confronting antisemitism and this is something that's actually a very big issue... and it's not actually accidental that some of the people who are struggling against Google and Amazon are some of the same people who are also protesting against Zionism and participating in this project called No Tech for Apartheid."

Claudia de la Cruz (Co-Executive Director of The People’s Forum):

  • "The people of Cuba, very similarly to the people of Palestine, have been able to survive because they've resisted, and they've resisted the same enemy, the colonial forces, the imperialist forces, the United States, and the illegal occupation of Israel."
  • "There's a lot of people the U.S. hangs out with that are not good for humanity - Israel is one of them, and so, what we're talking about [with] solidarity is also knowing who is our enemy."
  • "Just yesterday there was a bombing in Gaza [where] at least 15 children were killed. They were murdered, assassinated, killed by an immoral occupying force that receives financing equipment and all sorts of things from the enemies of the world because they are the enemies of the world, that's the U.S. government, state, and all these institutions. So when we're talking about Palestine, and we're talking about Gaza, we're talking about sisters and brothers, comrades, our people, our kin who are under the same threat and attack that the people in the hood of New York City [are under]. We have one of the biggest military forces which is the NYPD."
  • "[B]ecause that's what they do. They spread terrorism all over the world. Who tells Israel that it is a terrorist state that kills babies. Who does that?... When we're talking about colonial powers and imperial powers we're talking about people who have monopoly over violence, they have a monopoly on violence. They are the terrorists, they are the ones that are violent, they are the ones that have the economic political power to bend people to whatever their will is when people resist."
  • "[‘When we're talking about Gaza and Palestine’ we are talking] about a whole nation of people that is willing to die before they live under the boat of imperialism. A whole bunch of people that are willing to die to be seen and known as human beings. We can’t as people of consciousness oppose that resistance, we must support it... the recent attack on Gaza actually underlines that because Israel actually provoked and did the preemptive attack, preemptive bombing in order to provoke an attack and actually deny people the right to even speak up. So this was one of the things."

Bara (Video presenter at event):

  • "Israel continues to bomb Gaza indiscriminately killing civilians. It targets infrastructure, schools, houses, mosques, cars, and even animals. It wages a large a large-scale aggression against the besieged population thereby violating all international humanitarian laws and conventions.

Salma (Video presenter at event):

  • "A group of predators launched at you sitting and you don't know how to escape as if you are the next target. This is exactly what you feel every night during the Israelis wars against the Gaza strip. During the last three days it was announced that no one will survive. No one will survive the massacres of the Zionists, and whoever will survive from us will be a coincidence. Imagine that you already press any button in your home to turn it on just by pressing this button from the Israeli devices innocent souls are purified and departed from the whole world. Is this what you call peace? Is this what you call self-defense? We are living under a terrorist Israeli occupation and that Israeli occupation must be held accountable for its unjustified brutality and barbarity that targeted the Gaza strip during the past three days."

Unidentified Speaker (Video presenter at event):

  • "I mentioned it to folks and nobody there had heard about BDS or anything really about Palestine and it's really important for these struggles to be brought together. As I said I was part of the original group of Free Gaza movement. We took boats to Gaza that got there five times and then were attacked by Israel."

Hyder (Video presenter at event):

  • "As the Gaza blockade is entering its 15th year, I think we need to make sense of the present moment. It is of utmost importance for us here in Gaza Palestine to place this medieval siege imposed by apartheid Israel."
  • "And as if years of blockade interrupted by four genocidal wars is not enough, never before has a population been denied the basic requirements for survival as a deliberate policy of colonization, occupation and Apartheid. But hey, this is what Israel is doing to us today. Why is this allowed to happen in the 21st century?"
  • "Apartheid Israel has managed successfully, I would say, to transform the Gaza strip into a concentration and death camp, at the same time. Both a concentration and death camp where large numbers of people are detained and in which large numbers of prisoners are systematically killed under the watchful eye of either an indifferent international community or its full complicity and support."
  • "The end of last year Israel announced the completion of a kilometer sensor equipped underground wall around Gaza which includes hundreds of cameras radar and other sensors transforming Gaza into a concentration camp, a death camp. Indeed, being an openly Apartheid State, Israel has managed to demonize the people of Gaza simply because we are not born to Jewish mothers and therefore, we do not have the right to be treated as full human beings. Only those with white skin and or born to Jewish families can have that right in historic Palestine. The conclusion we have reached in Gaza is that Israel is intent on destroying Gaza altogether encouraged by the official world apathy to the suffering of the Palestinians."
  • "The destruction of Gaza will happen, definitely we can see it happening, and then the late Israeli prime minister Yitzhak Rabin’s dream will come true and we will disappear into the Mediterranean Sea. The late prime minister Yitzhak Rabin wished that one day he would wake up and fight and find out that all Gazans have drowned into the Mediterranean Sea, but we are resisting. With the support coming from the likes of our Cuban comrades and conscientious freedom-loving people, we will see the end of the last remaining apartheid system on earth. Long live the memory of comrade Fidel Castro, long live the memory of comrade Che Guevara, and long live the memory of all Palestinian martyrs. Long live Cuban Palestinian solidarity and friendship."

Classification: DEMONIZATION, DENYING JEWS SELF-DETERMINATION, CALLS FOR BDS
Short Description (New): The Department of Women’s, Gender, and Sexuality Studies of Rutgers University signed the Agitate Journal "Statement In Solidarity With The People Of Palestine" that demonized Israel, denied Jews self-determination, and called on the university to "divest from Israel, and support the call by Palestinian civil society for BDS" [1].
Description: The Department of Women’s, Gender, and Sexuality Studies of Rutgers University signed the Agitate Journal "Statement In Solidarity With The People Of Palestine" that demonized Israel, denied Jews self-determination, and called on the university to "divest from Israel, and support the call by Palestinian civil society for BDS" [1]. The statement read, "The Editorial Collective of AGITATE! Unsettling Knowledges, the Imagining Transnational Solidarities Research Circle (ITSRC), and the faculty in the Department of Gender, Women & Sexuality Studies at the University of Minnesota-Twin Cities stand in solidarity with the people of Palestine and condemn the Israeli state’s brutal settler colonial violence and ethnic cleansing of Palestinians... U.S. imperialism that engenders the maiming and killing of the Palestinian people by the U.S.-backed Israeli state. We insist on our ethical and political responsibility to raise our voices against settler colonialism, and the U.S. government’s enabling military and monetary support of the apartheid state of Israel. A recent Human Rights Watch report acknowledges that Israel continues to '[commit] crimes against humanity of apartheid and persecution.' The Israeli state’s forced removal of Palestinian families in the Sheikh Jarrah neighborhood in occupied East Jerusalem and the Israeli state’s disproportionate war against the people of Gaza are the most recent episodes of blatant state-orchestrated ethnic cleansing that has been going on for decades. Yet, the U.S. administration remains silent in the face of this massacre... we reaffirm our commitment to anti-colonialism, abolition, and the dismantlement of militarized state violence across the world. Like many U.S.-based universities, the University of Minnesota has investments in companies that do business in illegal Israeli settlements in Palestine. We ask our university to divest from Israel, and support the call by Palestinian civil society for BDS (Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions). As educators and cultural workers, we respond to the call by the Palestinian community in Lydda for international protection for Indigenous Palestinians from Israeli state-sanctioned pogroms. As scholars and solidarity workers who seek justice everywhere, we respond to the call of Palestinian feminists and Palestinian freedom fighters for transnational solidarity and assert that Palestine is a feminist issue. 'Freedom is a constant struggle' and none of us will be free unless the Palestinian people are free and Palestinian land is liberated. We stand with: the people of Palestine in their demand to end settler colonialism in occupied Palestine and their right of return; Native, Indigenous, and First Nations peoples in their struggles for liberation [and] land return... In Palestine, Kashmir, Minneapolis, Colombia, India and elsewhere, the dispossessed people’s protests against the tremendous forces of settler colonialism, brutal state repression and genocidal state apathy are repeatedly reduced to 'riot,' 'anti-nationalism,' 'national security threats,' or 'conflict' between two equal sides. We strongly reject the logic of 'both sides' when it comes to violent power that subjugates people and constructs them as 'terrorists', robbing them of their dignity and humanity and their right to struggle for justice. Lastly, we object to the curtailment of academic freedom when it comes to critiquing the apartheid state of Israel. The conflation of objections to the Israeli state’s settler colonial violence with anti-semitism is itself a violent oppressive form of censorship and an insult to our academic and moral integrity. We echo the South African anti-apartheid leader Archbishop Desmond Tutu and the Palestinian civil society who have rightly pointed out that 'If you are neutral in situations of injustice, you have chosen the side of the oppressor.' Free Palestine from ongoing settler colonial terror—now!" Link 1

Short Description (New): Center for Palestine Studies, Department of English, Lehman College, Center for Place, Culture, and Politics, CUNY Graduate Center, Program in Middle Eastern Studies, CUNY Graduate Center and the People’s Forum held the event "Autoethnography of a Palestinian Anthropologist" [1], where one speaker demonized Israel [2].
Description:
Center for Palestine Studies, Department of English, Lehman College, Center for Place, Culture, and Politics, CUNY Graduate Center, Program in Middle Eastern Studies, CUNY Graduate Center and the People’s Forum held the event "Autoethnography of a Palestinian Anthropologist" [1], where one speaker demonized Israel [2].

Rami Salameh (Assistant Professor in, and Chair of, the Department of Philosophy and Cultural Studies, as well as the Director of the Program in Contemporary Arab Studies, at Birzeit University):

  • "On the morning of 24th of April 2016, I was doing my ethnography as if it were any normal day out along the checkpoint until I heard sudden gunshots. There was a deep silence for a few seconds, followed by rapid and scared footsteps fleeing to escape death. At that moment I was not an ethnographer, I was a colonized body among other bodies trying to take shelter from bullets. On that day, both 23 year old Marram and 16 year old Ibrahim Taha, a sister and a brother, were shot dead by an Israeli soldier just for crossing the wrong path on the checkpoint."
  • "We are engaging with this world as a colonized subject. I was concerned with understanding the subjective experience of living under a settler colonial order, and what does it mean to live and to have a body in this part of the world?"
  • "My own experiences of field work studies of colonialism are often more concerned with analyzing and destroying central colonial power and it's modalities of control. A process of analyzing and engaging with this context could also and easily trap the researcher into double objectifying the colonized subjects. The colonial subjects are first objectified by colonial power, and then they are re-objectified by the researcher in the effort to deconstruct power. Take for example when studies have been on political prisoners and especially in hunger strikes, so the system of Israeli prisons strives mainly to control and subjugate Palestinian prisoners."
  • "As a Palestinian, and I don't mean being Palestinian as an identity, but rather being as someone who constantly being shaped by the colonial power as being an oppressed subject."

Description: Women’s Studies at CSU Dominguez Hills signed the statement "Gender Studies Departments in Solidarity with Palestinian Feminist Collective" that demonized Israel and denied Jews self-determination [1]. The statement read, "We stand in solidarity with the people of Palestine. We unequivocally answer and amplify the call from the Palestinian Feminist Collective for 'feminists everywhere to speak up, organize, and join the struggle for Palestinian liberation.'... We do not subscribe to a 'both sides' rhetoric that erases the military, economic, media, and global power that Israel has over Palestine. This is not a 'conflict' that is too 'controversial and complex' to assess. Israel is using violent force, punitive bureaucracy, and the legal system to expel Palestinians from their rightful homes and to remove Palestinian people from their land. Israeli law systematically discriminates against Palestinian citizens of Israel. Illegal Israeli settlements choke and police Palestinian communities, and Palestinians are cut off from each other by a network of checkpoints, laws, settler-only highways, and a separation wall that swallows illegally occupied Palestinian land. Both Human Rights Watch and B’Tselem have concluded that Israeli policies and practices towards Palestinians amount to apartheid. As gender studies departments in the United States, we are the proud benefactors of decades of feminist anti-racist, and anti-colonial activism that informs the foundation of our interdiscipline... [We] know that 'Palestine is a Feminist Issue.' In solidarity, we call for the end of Israel's military occupation of Palestine and for the Palestinian right to return to their homes. As residents, educators, and feminists who are also against the settler colonialism of the U.S., we refuse to normalize or accept the United States’ financial, military, diplomatic and political role in Palestinian dispossession. Furthermore, we will not tolerate any censorship of nor retribution against Palestinian scholars, activists, and those openly critical of the Israeli state. We join a vibrant, vast, and growing international solidarity community, composed of those raising their voices in support of Palestinian's right to freedom, return, safety, flourishing, and self-determination. May 15th marked the 73rd anniversary of the Nakba, an ongoing ethnic cleansing of Palestine... Palestinians have been resisting settler colonialism for more than one hundred years. We hail the fortitude and determination of the Palestinian people, who remain, despite the fragmentation of their populations, united in their demands to end their oppression." Link 1

Description: The student newspaper, The Daily Campus, published an article that demonized Israel and quoted students supporting BDS [1]. The article stated, "'Keep on supporting Palestine, and do not be afraid,' was heard on Tuesday, Nov. 2, as University of Connecticut students gathered on Fairfield Way to protest the Israeli takeover in Palestine, while UConn’s Hillel organization, center for Jewish student life at the university, set up a booth in silent opposition to the movement... 'Ditch companies and products that actively support the oppression of Palestinian people, say yes to Palestinian companies, say no to Lockheed Martin, Paypal and Caterpillar,' [a student] said during her speech. According to the BDS website, the movement is a Palestinian-led organization compiling multiple pro-Palestenian groups that support the movement’s cause and advocate for Palestinian freedom, justice and equality. [The student] said she was happy to speak at the rally about a cause she believes in. She said she enjoys speaking out about issues that are important to her. 'I spoke today because I just feel that no one at UConn knows what’s going on. No one knows how UConn is taking their tuition and funding it toward genocide,' [the student] said in an interview after the event... 'I would encourage students to look up BDS, look up the specific companies that we are boycotting and divesting from because a lot of people try and get caught up into like small companies that might support Israel but that’s not how we’re gonna make a change, that’s not how we’re gonna get attention,' [said the President of SJP]." Short Description (New): As part of “Palestine Awareness Week”, Harvard Palestine Solidarity Committee held the event “Settler Colonialism and Indigeneity - from Palestine to Turtle Island” [1], where both academic speakers engaged in rhetoric that demonized Israel and Zionists, negated Jewish claims of indigeneity, and denied Jewish self-determination [2].
Description:
As part of “Palestine Awareness Week”, Harvard Palestine Solidarity Committee held the event “Settler Colonialism and Indigeneity - from Palestine to Turtle Island” [1], where both academic speakers engaged in rhetoric that demonized Israel and Zionists, negated Jewish claims of indigeneity, and denied Jewish self-determination [2].

Professor Loubna Qutami (Assistant Professor in Department of Asian American Studies, UCLA):

  • "[Settler-colonialism] in Palestine... includes forced exodus of communities. It includes decimation of their landscapes, a decimation of their access to natural agricultural, water resources, the spread of infectious disease [and] genocidal killing campaigns. But aside from the physical removal element it also includes a very deep insidious cultural process right in order for a settler to naturalize their settlement in the land and to claim the land as their own as a form of property, not as a provider of life, which is a way that many indigenous communities have understood land through their cultural frameworks prior to conquest. But in order for settlers to naturalize their presence on land they also have to erase history because no person wants to say my country, my nation, this national identity that I stand for is built on genocide, it's built on looting and theft, it's built on a false identity. So, in order to claim that land naturally and comfortably settlers need to erase history, they need to erase the narratives of native peoples, they need to criminalize the narratives of native peoples, they need to decimate spiritual practices and cultural practices with land. And we've seen that transpire you know against many different indigenous nations, in Turtle Island as well as with Palestinians."
  • "We still see… the kind of physical forced removal of Palestinians from their lands, but we also still, if we think about it through a settler-colonial framework, we can understand why the backlash against the Palestine narrative or the Palestinian narrative is so visceral it's so aggressive and violent, because any Palestinian narrative that challenges the dominant narratives of Zionist colonial history, settler colonial history, challenges the very foundation of Zionist settlement, the very foundation of Zionist claims to the land themselves as indigenous inhabitants… that's something that's really important to understand about settler colonialism, because it's not a temporal project."
  • "[B]y 1948 when the Nakba happened, about 800 000 Palestinians were removed from their lands and that really sets the origins of the ongoing physical struggle for return and for freedom."
  • "[S]uch egregious violence happening to the Palestinians, to indigenous communities, to black communities… for me the conceptualization of that [decolonial] approach it has to have the sort of long-term slow de-colonial work of building a different community and a different vision and a different structure for the world that we want to live in, but we need the vibrant anti-colonial movement to dismantle the world that we live in now."
  • "In Palestine, for example, we talk about settler-colonialism as a project of forcible removal of Palestinians from the land acquiring the most amount of land with the least amount of Palestinians, and how the history of Zionist slogans, ‘a land for people for people without a land,’ ‘the old will die, the young will forget,’ was all driven toward completing that kind of settler-colonial utopic vision of a land without Palestinians. But the racial, colonial, militarized occupation of the Gaza Strip and the West Bank, for example, signify to us that even some people will say it's just an occupation until they're able to get rid of all the Palestinians on that land, but they signify to us that settler-colonialism can very much be an intersecting system with racial capitalism or with racial colonialism. Palestinians are subjects of captivity, they are subjects of imprisonment, they are subjects of siege in the West Bank, their lands are divided between concrete walls that are 25 feet high and militarized checkpoints and roadblocks and security outposts, and that is a very profitable occupation for the Israeli economy because testing those weapons on Palestinian bodies, those control and surveillance technologies on Palestinian lands and bodies [which] is then exported to other sites of political and racial and colonial repression and violence across the world whether it's in India or it's in Brazil or now in Greece, in the Mediterranean militarizing, the Mediterranean borrowing from these Israeli technologies and some of those technologies are reappearing here in the streets of the United States. The cooperation between police departments and Israel is one example… the United States has long existed as a project of settler colonial, white supremacy, state violence, but these [two] systems [of the U.S. and Israel], the actors of these systems, the ideologies that undergird these systems - they have deeply intimate relationships."
  • [In response to the question, “I’ve been seeing a lot online in response to the recent uprisings and even Indigenous People's Day, I've seen a lot of Zionists... appropriating a lot of that language to bolster their own movements, and so I was wondering, how do we prevent or protect the language of resistance - that is luckily becoming more well-known and popular - from being appropriated and subverted in that way?”]: "Thanks for asking that question. I'm not surprised that there's an appropriation. The thing about Zionism is that as an ideology, it somehow has attracted folks from across the political spectrum. So, there's liberal Zionists, there's far right-wing Zionists, there are Zionists who are progressive on pretty much every issue on the earth, on the planet, with the exception of Palestine. Which of course indicates to us that they're not truly progressive, because that exception can't really mean that you're advocating for a truly progressive line. But I think that there is something particularly tricky with the way that Zionists appropriate indigeneity as a claim to Palestine. If we look at the late 1800s and early 1900’s, Zionist very easily branded themselves and very comfortably branded themselves as a colonial movement, and once the world order started to shift and colonialism was kind of being threatened by political movements across the third world who were ousting colonial regimes, the Zionists, early Zionists, very quickly abandoned their overt claim to their project as a colonial one and they started to articulate their project as an anti-colonial struggle in a way. They paralleled it to the anti-colonial struggle of India against the British. By World War II, they articulated it as the only solution to reverse the horrors of antisemitism in Europe, of the violence and extermination of Jewish communities of Nazi Germany and the Third Reich, that this was the only solution. So, I think that there's something really important about the way that their narratives have evolved over time, because once settlers arrived to Palestine and once the State of Israel was formed in 1948 and continued carrying out land annexations and so forth, we started to really see a more vigilant effort by Zionists to claim that they were the chosen people of G-d, who are indigenously from this land, who are making their rightful return. And that also is complicated, because it doesn't just fall in within the indigenous framework, it even follows within some of the ways that refugee law or narratives on refugeehood are so deeply tied to the complex relationship between what it means to be a refugee and what it means to be a settler. Some of the early organizations who were helping the migration of Jewish communities [to] come to Palestine, settle the land, [urging] Zionists to come and settle the land, they were articulating their work as a philanthropic organization that was doing refugee resettlement. So this is very complicated because it is not uncommon for Zionists to appropriate liberal progressive talking points and systems of law, whether it's refugee law or genocide law or indigeneity as a framework, in order to mask their project and in order to safeguard their project from being named for what it is, which is a settler-colonial enterprise that started officially in 1948 in the exact moment that the world was overturning colonial regimes across the world. So, I think that's what I would say in response to that."
  • [Reading a quote by an activist]: "[W]e [the United states] are one huge settlement on stolen land we can't talk about Israel because we are Israel. And I just wanted to say that despite those feelings every now and then that we think Israel is the biggest, most mightiest, most powerful, most aggressive country in the world and [that] Zionism is unbreakable, the movement and its repression is signifying the opposite, and it also is signifying our ability to actually work through [the] process of decolonization here in Turtle Island, because the United States is Israel."

Professor Marcelo Felipe Garzo Montalvo (Visiting Assistant Professor of Latinx Studies, Harvard University):

  • "[T]hat's why it's so important that we take control of the narrative. We do the cultural production work that reasserts why that doesn't make sense, why a Zionist appropriation of Indigenous People's Day doesn't make sense. So, it's important to continue to articulate just a good old refusal and resistance and critique and an understanding, an alternative, of why that is problematic. But at the same time, I want to offer that I think that in that case, the Zionists, those Zionist organizations who are doing that, might not fully understand their own implications of what Indigenous People's Days means. They might not even make that connection for themselves that we're making here today, that Zionism is a form of settler-colonialism and therefore it is antithetical to Indigenous People's Day, and so they may not even have that comprehension."

Short Description (New): SJP created materials and posted them on Instagram in order for students and supporters to learn "about common misconceptions on divestment and to better understand what UTDivest is all about," where the information contained rhetoric that demonized Israel and belittled Jewish student concerns with antisemitism and feeling unsafe [1].
Description: SJP created materials and posted them on Instagram in order for students and supporters to learn "about common misconceptions on divestment and to better understand what UTDivest is all about," where the information contained rhetoric that demonized Israel and belittled Jewish student concerns with antisemitism and feeling unsafe [1]. SJP claimed under the heading, "Is Divestment Anti Semitic?" that "As students, we strongly condemn anti-Semitism, which is contrary to the principles of equality, justice, and human rights for which we are fighting. Our only aim is to end Israeli policies and practices that abuse human rights and violate international law. BDS is a rights-based movement and its goal is to achieve justice and equality. BDS opposes all forms of racism and oppression, including anti-Semitism. It specifically targets divestment from oppressive companies, not Jewish people. Military occupation has nothing to do with Judaism." Under the heading, "Does Divestment Create an Unsafe Space for Jewish Students?" SJP stated, "All students' safety must be protected. It is important to distinguish between being unsafe and being uncomfortable. It might be unpleasant for some students to hear facts about Israeli oppression or criticism of the policies of the Israeli state, but discomfort is not the same thing as threats to safety." SJP continued, "Isn't Divestment Controversial? Divestment only asks for compliance with human rights and international law- a demand that frankly should not be controversial. Nevertheless, BDS has been becoming more mainstream as evidenced by numerous similar student-led divestment initiatives targeting corporations involved in human rights abuses and other transgressions including an anti-Apartheid movement. The campaign to divest from companies complicit in Israeli war crimes was initiated in 2005 when Palestinian civil society called upon people of conscience all over the world to launch broad boycotts, to implement divestment initiatives, and to demand sanctions against Israel, until Palestinian rights are recognized in full compliance with international law. It only asks that we wipe our hands clean of human rights violations and calls for compliance with international law. We are forced to take a position since our university invests in these companies." Link 1

Short Description (New): SJP Rutgers-Newark held the event "The Reality of the IDF" with Eran Efrati [1], where Efrati demonized Israel and encouraged students to engage in BDS, including an academic boycott of Israel [2].
Description:
SJP Rutgers-Newark held the event "The Reality of the IDF" with Eran Efrati [1], where Efrati demonized Israel and encouraged students to engage in BDS, including an academic boycott of Israel [2].

Eran Efrati:

  • "This [in Hebron] is an apartheid street. And although I didn’t really understand what apartheid means at that time as a soldier it’s gradually starting to creep up on me that what I’m doing is not defending my family. What I’m doing is just segregating and separating two kinds of human beings and controlling just one of them."
  • "And all of a sudden I understand that when I was trying to look for being on the right side of history, all of my life when I decided that I would never do these things [that the Nazis did to Jews that] I read about as a kid, when I was trying to understand [the] psyche of German soldiers to understand how to defend my country better, I didn’t pay attention to the fact that gradually, slowly, I became that [Nazi] man. I understood that you cannot just be a human being, a loving human being, a caring human being to your family to your friends, but do that during the day. Doing occupation and apartheid during the day."
  • "I started to go into activism and trying to understand how can we stop the apartheid in Palestine, this segregation between Jews and Palestinians and what I realized is Israel took this teargas canister and made it - by their official language - the most deadliest teargas canister ever in the world... No matter who Israel did business with around the world or sell their weapons around the world after every operation in Gaza, after every operation in the West Bank, the US kept popping up as a supporter and a lot of money was involved. A lot. We were testing weapons and then selling them and that understanding completely changed my life. The idea that I as a soldier was testing weapons in a laboratory on Palestinians."
  • "Campus security from different universities were traveling to Israel to train with the military, with the police on occupied territory... and this is something that happened every year multiple times a year. They see the weapons that are being used and [Israel is] training [them in] special techniques [that] Israel enhanced during the 70 years occupation of Palestinians."
  • "It’s so basic to our identity…separation. If you look like this you go to this line... this is what the state of Israel is based on. We build a full country out of this idea. Nobody needs an explanation. When researchers or journalists are talking about racial profiling …the best way to understand it here is through the airport system... the truth is it’s just a racist tactic of everyone that is looking like you would be sent into a different line and everybody that looks white, European, Jewish, goes to stand in a different one."
  • "30 billion dollars... a year the United States is giving Israel in military aid to basically 'protect herself', or enforce apartheid in Palestine."
  • "Basically how we’re doing [activism is through] effective BDS campaigns -- boycott, divestment and sanctions against the state of Israel, but it starts right here locally at home... we’re working on [the] academic part of it. All the universities that have ties to universities in Israel that are effectively a part of the Israeli occupation of Palestine, you can find it and use us for research and guidance a little bit, to help us build a local campaign."
  • "I do believe that state violence and police brutality is targeting [brown people]... they learn [from Israel] that the enemy is brown or black. They obviously knew that before they didn’t need Israel to teach them that, but Israel is enhancing that idea to the maximum. When the law enforcement comes back home [after training in Israel], they’re looking for you, and the only way to resist it is to understand that they’re joining forces to oppress us. We need to start joining together to resist them. For me that starts with the Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions campaign against Israel. We’re working very closely with BNC, the Boycott National Committee in Ramallah, also with the academia part right now, and we would love to work with you as a school about the academic crimes you have for Rutgers with the Israeli government, Israeli universities and about how we can stop both the oppression in Palestine, but also racial justice in the US together."

Short Description (New): Yalies for Palestine released a statement [1] that condoned the massacre, maiming, rape, torture and kidnapping of thousands of Israeli civilians [2-3], expressing support for these behaviors by calling them Palestinian "resistance" and blaming Israel for the violence, claiming: "We hold the Israeli Zionist regime responsible for the unfolding violence and denounce the Israeli occupation, apartheid system, and its military rule;" "The events of October 7th are the inevitable outcome of a decades-long apartheid and suffocating blockade that continues to escalate;" "Yalies4Palestine stands in full support of the Palestinian people's right to resist colonization and return to their land;" and "Breaking out of a prison requires force".
Description: Yalies for Palestine released a statement [1] that condoned the massacre, maiming, rape, torture and kidnapping of thousands of Israeli civilians [2-3], expressing support for these behaviors by calling them Palestinian "resistance" and blaming Israel for the violence, claiming: "We hold the Israeli Zionist regime responsible for the unfolding violence and denounce the Israeli occupation, apartheid system, and its military rule;" "The events of October 7th are the inevitable outcome of a decades-long apartheid and suffocating blockade that continues to escalate;" "Yalies4Palestine stands in full support of the Palestinian people's right to resist colonization and return to their land;" and "Breaking out of a prison requires force". The full statement read: "Yalies4Palestine stands in unwavering support of the Palestinian people's right to resist colonial oppression. We hold the Israeli Zionist regime responsible for the unfolding violence and denounce the Israeli occupation, apartheid system, and its military rule. The events of October 7th are the inevitable outcome of a decades-long apartheid and suffocating blockade that continues to escalate. For 16 years, millions of Palestinians have lived in the open-air prison that is Gaza, where they have suffered a land, sea, and air blockade that restricts movement and amounts to collective punishment that blatantly violates international law. The Zionist government has structured and securitized every aspect of Palestinian life for over 75 years. Palestinians have been forced to exist in a state of slow death, watching their land and people be taken from them. Yalies4Palestine stands in full support of the Palestinian people's right to resist colonization and return to their land. Previous nonviolent acts of resistance through boycotts, strikes, and protests are only met with criminalization, repression, and murder. Breaking out of a prison requires force, not desperate appeals to the colonizer or the 'international community' which has maintained the status quo as long as it is Palestinians paying the price. We stand with all colonized people fighting for their liberation from Palestine, to Armenia, to Kashmir, to Turtle Island, and beyond. On Monday, October 9, we call on our allies and the Yale and New Haven community to join us for Indigenous Peoples' Day to uplift the calls of the resistance, stand firm against colonialism, and rally at 3:00 PM at New Haven City Hall." Link 1 (Accessed: 10/9/23), Link 2 (Accessed: 10/9/23), Link 3 (Accessed: 10/9/23)

Description:
AMED at SFSU and Race and Resistance Studies held the teach-in event “Colonial Geographies * Border Crossing - Kashmir*Mexico*Palestine” [1] as part of AMED’s “open classrooms” that was livestreamed on the AMED at SFSU Facebook page and included rhetoric denying Jews self-determination and demonizing Israel and Zionists [2, 3].

AMED Director Rabab Abdulhadi:

  • “The Israeli law of return… bestows automatic citizenship on Jews arriving in Israel while denying indigenous Palestinian population the right to return to Palestine and to the homes from which they have been uprooted since 1948.”
  • “Israel then decides that we need more space to have the apartheid wall.”
  • “Israel does raids every single night. Every single night, they go and arrest people. Every single night. So, it’s not actually like a sovereign [state]. There’s no sovereignty.”
  • “You also need to fight… the whole Zionist movement that is basically trying to cut off and now trying to claim ethnic studies.”
  • “A lot of the slaves within the Muslim empire were Eastern European because… it wasn’t about the color of the skin. This wasn’t the racism attached to the color of the skin… later on, [when it] was very much attached to the colonialism… and the settler colonialism… part of it is very, very much connected to the story of Orientalism and Zionism. Very, very much connected.”

Saliem Shehadeh:

  • “In my organizing space with the Zionists they try to stop conversations by saying for many Jews Zionism is an integral part of our identity and they’re trying to use it they’re trying to deploy that to try and stop conversation. It’s the same thing… [with] South Africa and the whole debates about settlement post-apartheid is like… the white settlers… are saying they belong there… and it’s this sort of discourse that gets co-opted by the colonizers… It’s the same language it’s the same words that they’re using.”
  • "I actually want to begin with the chapter on building the Jewish state… So that chapter’s about specifically what’s happening post 1948… the context behind this is you have a Zionist settler colonial project [that] begins in the end of the 19th century [and takes a] massive hold by the early 20th century… Zionist settlers going into Palestine [the] beginning [of a] wave of accumulation of land dispossessing Palestinians…. the Nakba it’s commemorated on one date but it’s months and months and years and years long process as we argue it’s continuing today."
  • "How does [Israel] cement and keep hold of that settler colonial project and how does it keep hold of keeping the Palestinians out of Palestine and preventing them from returning and this is what they mean by building the Jewish state. So it’s a matter of continued dispossession…. Palestinians from 1948 to 1966 lived under… Marshall law… we see this akin to apartheid South Africa. And so, this was a way they also prevented refugees from coming back… Many have argued that this was also a mode of capitalist exploitation.”

Professor Blanca Misse:

  • “Of course I know Zionism is a movement of liberation of the oppressed Jews (laughter. Shehadeh laughs and shakes his head), and everybody feels they belong… [but] if you want me to recognize that you belong to this land too, you need to start by recognizing that I belong to this land too, which means concretely dismantling the state [of Israel] that is negating us.”

Professor Leslie Quintanilla:

  • “What happens when certain folks aren’t supposed to be there and the land doesn’t actually want you to be there or the waters don’t actually want you to be there? And I think about [that]. I remember seeing somewhere a post around the intense heat and sunburn that Israelis have experienced (laughing as she tells this) and high cancer rates, high skin cancer rates, it’s like, yes the land doesn’t [want you], right, you don’t belong in that land, you’re too pale, go away!”

Description: SJP relaunched their campaign to de-shelve all Sabra products [1], which was announced on Parrish Porch, where signatures were collected on SJP's petition [2] and stickers were handed out for students to place on Sabra products in the dining areas around campus stating, "This product funds apartheid". The petition stated, "Swarthmore College must stop the sale of all Sabra products, currently available at Kohlberg Coffee Bar, Sci Center Coffee Bar, and Essie Mae’s. Sabra Hummus is owned by the Strauss Group, a multinational corporation that invests in, funds, and even sends care packages to the Golani Brigade of the Israeli armed forces. Even by the abysmal human rights standards of the Israeli Occupation Forces (IOF), the Golani brigade is particularly brutal... It is morally unacceptable that Swarthmore should continue to support the international human rights violations of the Golani Brigade and Israeli Apartheid at large. By selling Sabra, Swarthmore is an accessory to the occupation of Palestine. We call upon President Smith to affirm the dignity of Palestinian life, recognize the illegal occupation of Palestinian land, and de-shelve all current Sabra products and end their future sale at Swarthmore College." Short Description (New): SJP held the rally "Stop Israel's Annihilation of Gaza" [1] where History Professor Russell Rickford referred to Hamas' incursion into Israel resulting in the massacre, torture, kidnapping and maiming of thousands of Israeli civilians as "exhilarating" [2-3].
Description: SJP held the rally "Stop Israel's Annihilation of Gaza" [1] where History Professor Russell Rickford referred to Hamas' incursion into Israel resulting in the massacre, torture, kidnapping and maiming of thousands of Israeli civilians as "exhilarating" [2-3]. Remarks of Professor Rickford included: "Hamas has challenged the monopoly of violence. And in those first few hours, even as horrific acts were being carried out, many of which we would not learn about until later, there are many Gazans of good will, many Palestinians of conscience, who abhor violence, as do you, as do I. Who abhor the targeting of civilians, as do you, as do I. Who were able to breathe, they were able to breathe for the first time in years. It was exhilarating. It was energizing. And if they weren’t exhilarated by this challenge to the monopoly of violence, by this shifting of the balance of power, then they would not be human. I was exhilarated. What has Hamas done? Hamas has shifted the balance of power. Hamas has punctured the illusion of invincibility. That’s what they have done. You don’t have to be a Hamas supporter to recognize that. Hamas has changed the terms of the debate. Israeli officials are right — nothing will be the same again." Professor Rickford doubled down on his comments following the rally in commentary to the Cornell Sun [3], stating, "What I was referring to is in those first few hours, when they broke through the apartheid wall, that it seemed to be a symbol of resistance, and indeed a new phase of resistance in the Palestinian struggle. We are acutely aware of the devastation, the daily destruction and degradation caused by Israeli policies, caused by Israeli apartheid, caused by the occupation. So in that context, this act of defiance of boring across the wall was a significant symbol. It really signaled that the Palestinian will to resist had not been broken. In subsequent days, we learned of some of the horrifying realities. I want to make it clear that Hamas is a fundamentalist organization. It’s important to note that in some ways, the fundamentalism of Hamas mirrors that of Israeli leadership." Link 1 (Accessed: 10/17/23), Link 2 (Accessed: 10/17/23), Link 3 (Accessed: 10/17/23)

Description: California Scholars for Academic Freedom wrote [1] Chancellor Breland to condemn the Chancellor for an email sent criticizing a letter [2] published and circulated by SWANA that demonized Israel, where the letter from California Scholars for Academic Freedom demonized Israel as well. The letter by cs4af stated, "We write on behalf of California Scholars for Academic Freedom (cs4af) in response to your May 26 email criticizing the statement by North Orange County Community College District’s Southwest Asian and North African (SWANA) faculty and staff association that discussed the Israeli army’s killing, on May 11, of journalist Shireen Abu Akleh... At cs4af, we know too well that Zionists and Zionist organizations, in their desperate efforts to shield the Israeli state from accountability for its ongoing and pervasive harms to the rights and lives of Palestinians, regularly lodge dishonest charges of ‘antisemitism’ at speech critical of the state. Unless these Zionist attacks on academic freedom are resisted on a principled basis, the result is a discriminatory Palestinian exception to speech rights and academic freedom protections... Here one also should note that the world’s two leading human rights organizations—both Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch—have recently released meticulously documented reports concluding that the Israeli state is a regime of apartheid from the Jordan River to Mediterranean Sea. If you have not yet read these reports, we urge you to do so... Cs4af thus calls upon you to rescind that May 26 email and to restore the posting of the SWANA statement, on an immediate basis. Failure to take these two remedial steps will leave academic freedom at NOCCCD greatly impaired and, on that basis, will expose your vitally important colleges and programs to continuing and escalating reputational harm." Description: The Student Senate published a statement to "extend our condolences to those affected by the current crisis within Palestine" that demonized Israel and encouraged BDS. The statement claimed, "It is possible for us to criticize the violence done at the hands of the Israeli government currently headed by Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. This violent ethnic cleansing has been happening since 1948 and continues to this very day... The United States of America's compliance and support of ethnic cleansing in Palestine. We urge you to text 'resist' to 50409 to contact your representative and hold our government accountable for using taxpayer dollars to promote violence. Furthermore, please advocate and support bills such as H.R. 2590 to stop our tax dollars from funding ethnic cleansing. To learn more about this crisis... free resources are available from the following organizations: Jewish Voice for Peace, Palestine Youth Movement, Eye on Palestine, Decolonize Palestine, [and] Wayne State's student organization Students for Justice in Palestine also provides resources tailored for students. Organizations to donate to: Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions (BDS), an organization working to end international support of Palestinian oppression. If you are unable to donate, you can still use your voice to educate others and amplify the voice of Palestinians. You can also participate in BDS which focuses on not buying products that contribute to the crisis... We understand that this statement does not remedy all injustice perpetrated against the Palestinian people. This message is to show those affected that they are seen, cared for, and supported by the Student Senate. Allyship is an imperative and silence is not neutrality. In solidarity, WSU Student Senate." Link 1
University Response: President Wilson released a statement that read, "This week, our Student Senate published a statement regarding the current Israeli/Palestinian conflict on the senate’s social media. The statement was issued after considerable deliberation among the student senators, not all of whom agreed with the position expressed. Some members of the community have assumed that the statement carries the endorsement of the university. It does not. We are a university community, and we support the right to free speech. While we support the right of our students to express views important to them, I regret their use of some needlessly inflammatory terminology. We must treat each other with civility and respect. Some of their words did not do that."

Short Description (New): The anti-Zionist group Not In Our Name CUNY issued [1] a "Statement of Solidarity with SJP UW Madison" that was promoted with commentary of "#ZionismOutOfUWMadison" and "#zionismoutofcuny", where the statement demonized Israel and denigrated Jewish groups and Jewish Zionist identity [2].
Description: The anti-Zionist group Not In Our Name CUNY issued [1] a "Statement of Solidarity with SJP UW Madison" that was promoted with commentary of "#ZionismOutOfUWMadison" and "#zionismoutofcuny", where the statement demonized Israel and denigrated Jewish groups and Jewish Zionist identity [2]. The statement read, "We at CUNY Not In Our Name (NION), a Jewish anti-Zionist collective at the City University of New York, stand unequivocally with the driven and outspoken members of Students for Justice in Palestine at UW-Madison. The manufactured moral panic over an incident falsely deemed antisemitic–chalk markings on a campus pavement that equate Zionism with racism and genocide–serves to minimize and weaponize real lived Jewish trauma, while at the same time aiming to silence people who are organizing for Palestinian liberation. Within the past three or so decades, forces in support of Zionism have pushed the myth that anti-Zionism is antisemitic... Ironically, this coordinated attack by multiple Zionist organizations on one student-run club shows how successful the students at SJP UW-Madison are and draws new attention to their powerful campus organizing for the liberation of Palestine... As it has been accurately assessed and defined by Palestinians and the earliest Jewish anti-Zionists, we reiterate: Zionism is not a synonym for the Jewish people, faith, culture, ethnic identity, religious identity, or history. Zionism is a 150-year-old political ideology built with inspiration from European colonialism, ethnic nationalism, and white supremacy since its birth. Anyone, Jewish or not, can be a Zionist. And anyone, Jewish or not, can oppose Zionism. The weaponization and obfuscation of antisemitism is intended to confuse both Jewish and non-Jewish people and to scare people from speaking out against the settler colonial state of 'Israel', which is aligned with and supported by US imperialism. This is the direct result of the ongoing attempt to re-define antisemitism since the early 1990s in a way that conflates Jewish identity with the national pseudo-identity project of 'Israeli.' Tellingly, the same organizations that target those working in solidarity with Palestine liberation rarely speak out against actual examples of antisemitism... Paradoxically, by diverting attention from and not calling out actual antisemitism, these groups strengthen antisemitism, making it harder for Jews and our allies to fight against real prejudice. Instead, false claims of antisemitism are made against Palestinians and their allies, leading to surveillance, systematic persecution, and harassment, and causing distress in their personal lives, academic pursuits, and careers. Little, if anything at all, is being done to protect SJP students from targeted harassment by organizations such as StopAntisemitism, the Anti Defamation League, Jewish on Campus, and college clubs like Hillel and Badger Alliance for Israel. Real and current actions of harm against Palestinian students and their allies are being intentionally disregarded as Madison for Palestine describe in their own statement. Jewish on Campus’s recent statement mentions blood libel, a real antisemitic myth historically and modernly used by European, and now American, Christians to justify horrific pogroms and ethnic cleansings of Jewish people. Yet here this trope is used–in a common tactic–to slander student activists for accurately assessing the realities of Zionism that Palestinians face. These campus clubs are not being targeted on the basis of their Jewish identity; that claim is fictitious. Instead, they are being criticized and held accountable on the grounds that they regularly endorse programs as well as trips to the settler colonial state, continuing ties that aid in the surveillance, censorship, and harassment of Palestinian, Black, and anti-Zionist student organizers. These clubs maintain alliances with organizations with histories of distorting or working against progressive movements as documented by the International Jewish Anti-Zionist Network. As a collective of people who are fighting for Palestinian liberation on CUNY campuses, we see our struggle inherently linked with the SJP at UW-Madison and have seen these tactics deployed to many of our members and our colleagues. It is imperative that we stand together. The SJP at UW-Madison students should not be forced to engage or defend themselves against an obvious smear campaign. Demands for the freedom of occupied peoples is an inherently just and principled cause which should be met with the utmost defense from the administration of UW-Madison. We hope that the students, faculty, administration, and chancellor of UW-Madison stand in solidarity with SJP UW-Madison and their allies as we fight for the same on CUNY campuses. We encourage other Jewish anti-Zionist and anti-racist organizations to act against this blatant act of censorship. Not in our name! Solidarity from New York City, CUNY NION" Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): Students United for Palestinian Equal Rights - OSU (SUPER-OSU) created [1] and circulated [2] a petition for students to sign to "REMOVE Course HST 490/590 'Israeli Society and Pluralism' at Oregon State University," that demonized Israel throughout.
Description: Students United for Palestinian Equal Rights - OSU (SUPER-OSU) created [1] and circulated [2] a petition for students to sign to "REMOVE Course HST 490/590 'Israeli Society and Pluralism' at Oregon State University," that demonized Israel throughout. The petition read, "This course is being offered for the Spring 2020 term at OSU. It will remove the narrative of the Palestinians and effectively work to normalize Israel’s ongoing ethnic cleansing of Palestinians as well as the Israeli occupation of Palestinian land. This course will put forth a skewed narrative that diminishes the reality of the occupation, ongoing ethnic cleansing, and genocide of the Palestinians. It’s critical that Oregon State University revoke its offering of the course because of the unjust and unethical narrative it will propagate. Students participating in this course without background knowledge of both sides of the conflict will only be exposed to a Zionist narrative that claims Israel is an excellent example of pluralism, when in reality it is if you are non-Palestinian only. For these and other reasons, we demand that Oregon State University and the School of History, Philosophy, and Religion remove this course immediately." The petition continued with "Further Background" that states, "Israel is governed by a far-right, racist government that has occupied and colonized Palestinian land for more than 50 years, and continues to ethnically cleanse Palestinians, stealing what little land they have left, for Israeli Jewish settlers (settler-colonialism). Today there are more than seven million Palestinian refugees and Israel continues to refuse them their right to return to their homes. Palestinians are subject to severe oppression and apartheid conditions, yet the course description claims that Israel tries to reach a 'modus vivendi' with the Palestinians both within Israel's borders and even in the Palestinian territory that Israel occupies. Ultimately, the base claim of this course is that Israel is a pluralistic society, but it is only a pluralistic society for Jewish people and certainly not for the native people of the land, the Palestinians." Link 1, Link 2
