Short Description (New): A number of departments co-sponsored a two-day conference [1] called “Rights and Wrongs - A Constitution and Citizenship Day Conference,” where a panel on the first day entitled "The Palestinian Exception?" involved panelists engaging in the demonization of Israel and denigrating remarks about Zionists on and off campus [2].
Description:
A number of departments co-sponsored a two-day conference [1] called “Rights and Wrongs - A Constitution and Citizenship Day Conference,” where a panel on the first day entitled "The Palestinian Exception?" involved panelists engaging in the demonization of Israel and denigrating remarks about Zionists on and off campus [2]. The panelists included professors Lisa Rofel, David Palumbo-Liu and Sang Hea Kil.

Lisa Rofel:

  • “It is unseemly to be angry let alone question the Israeli occupation of Palestinian land. Calling this occupation, ‘settler colonialism’, can induce severe sanctions by universities after an array of Zionist groups have weighed in. I have personally experienced this harassment… In my experience of harassment I received a thousand emails motivated by Tammi Benjamin who is the main force behind AMCHA that accused me of being a Nazi. [AMCHA note – defamatory and untrue remarks].”
  • “Until recently the charge of anti-Semitism against Jews was something that these right-wing Zionists tried to finesse. For example Tammi Benjamin was [upset about] events I organized on my campus [and used them as] prime examples of how our campus is antisemitic. Naturally she never mentions that every single one of the participants in my events which question Zionism were Jewish. Now she doesn’t care. She and Trump and other right-wingers who are in my view incredibly anti-Semitic are quite willing to charge those of us Jews who are critical of Israel of being anti-Semitic thus conflating anti-Semitism with political views of Israel.”
  • “The statement that Zionists are welcome on this campus is… antisemitic since again it assumes that all Jews are Zionists. What about those of us Jews that are non-Zionists or anti-Zionists? To say that American Jews must surely tow the line of the Israeli state is antisemitic as it brings up the whole history of antisemitism about Jews' so-called dual loyalty. This kind of antisemitism lines up with right-wing politics that bring together US white supremacists, Christian Zionists, and Jewish organizations to take the position that I call Israel no matter what, that is Israel no matter what it does. I cannot think of another country whose military is allowed on our campuses in the United States, but such is the case with Israel. Not only do they enter our campuses but they harass our students who speak out in favor of Palestinian rights.”
  • “… something is deeply wrong in America when our government, our political leaders, our media leaders, our university leaders, try to suppress debate about Israel and in effect support Israel’s efforts to destroy Palestinian’s lives.”
  • “What we also need to do is insist upon our academic freedom to develop a social justice agenda, one that calls out what Israel is actually doing: ethnic cleansing and apartheid.”

David Palumbo-Liu

  • “… the consequences of the actions that Zionism calls for it not just allows but it mandates it are against every notion of international law that we have right. And that’s not debatable. Those are things to that are the truth and yet put that forward often gets you in hot water because we’re told we’re denying the [self determination] of [the Jewish] people. Right… We don’t accept money from tobacco companies at least not up front. We don’t take money from petroleum interests because there’s a very good well-established connection between funding and truth which is produced under the mandate standard. So why not the same with regards to Zionism which supports precisely the mandate that the current regime in Israel is fulfilling?”

Sang Hea Kil

  • “I’ve had a long of history with Jonathan Roth [a Jewish professor at San Jose State University]. I think he seems to be the Zionist gate keeper on my campus…”

The event was co-sponsored by the Department of History, College of Liberal and Creative Arts California Faculty Association—SFSU Chapter, College of Ethnic Studies, College of Extended Learning, Division of Graduate Studies, Documentary Film Institute, History Students Association, Paul K. Longmore Institute on Disability, Modern Languages and Literatures Department, Paralegal Studies Program, Philosophy Department, Political Science Department, Sociology and Sexuality Studies Department, and Women and Gender Studies Department.

Link 1, Link 2

Classification: SUPPRESSION OF SPEECH/MOVEMENT/ASSEMBLY, DEMONIZATION, DENYING JEWS SELF-DETERMINATION, CALLS FOR BDS
Short Description (New): SJP released a statement urging the John Carroll Boler College of Business to "reconsider" the program's study abroad trip to Israel that was signed by Philip Metres, Professor of English and Director of the Peace, Justice And Human Rights Program, along with seven student groups[1].
Description: SJP released a statement urging the John Carroll Boler College of Business to "reconsider" the program's study abroad trip to Israel that was signed by Philip Metres, Professor of English and Director of the Peace, Justice And Human Rights Program, along with seven student groups[1]. The statement read: "As members of Students for Justice in Palestine, we condemn John Carroll's Boler College of Business trip to Israel. Here's why. As members of the Students for Justice in Palestine, we write to express our deep concern about John Carroll's Boler College of Business' proposed study group to Israel. While we understand the intention may be to provide a unique educational experience for students, we believe it is vital to consider the ethical and political implications of such a trip, especially since it confers John Carroll university graduate courses credit and therefore represents our academic reputation. The Palestinian people are currently living under an illegal military occupation, siege, blockade and apartheid. Palestinians have asked for an academic boycott of Israel as a means of protesting against the ongoing violation of their human rights and freedoms. We believe that participation in any study abroad program to Israel that ignores the realities that Palestinians face risks being complicit with the illegal and immoral occupation, colonization and apartheid that Israel has imposed on them. [Claim:] ‘BUT THIS IS NOT A POLITICAL TRIP’. [SJP response:] Israel's technology sector is not separate from its political and military actions. In fact, many of the technological advancements made in Israel are used to maintain the illegal occupation, ongoing oppression, and apartheid in Palestine. One runs the risk of being complicit in these actions by going on a trip to explore Israel's technology. We have been made aware that the tour will be visiting Israel's best technological companies, including Google, a company that has been doing business with apartheid, and whose technology has been used by the Israeli government and military to make the apartheid regime more efficient, violent and deadly for Palestinians. One example is how Google's cloud services may also be used to enable Israel's expansion of illegal settlements, thereby supporting discriminatory policies that expand segregated Jewish settlements while limiting the growth of Palestinian communities. We must recognize the ongoing violations of human rights and international law that occur every day by the Israeli government to the Palestinian people. Palestinians are denied fundamental freedoms such as freedom of movement, access to water and electricity, the right to own land, right to return, right to self determination and human dignity. Israel reinforces these oppressive measures through technological advancements... We urge John Carroll's Boler College of Business to reconsider this study group to Israel, and to recognize that typical travel and tourism, business trip and study abroad programs can contribute to ongoing colonization, gentrification, appropriation of native culture, and the violation of international law. Resisting racism means listening to the voices of those impacted by these systems of harm rather than normalizing and contributing to those systems through tourism or study abroad programs. We implore you to educate yourself on the illegal occupation, ongoing oppression, and apartheid in Palestine. As members of a community dedicated to justice and equity, we must recognize the role we play in perpetuating oppression. We ask that you reconsider this trip and instead seek out ways to support Palestinian rights and promote justice for all people. Thank you for your attention to this important matter... To learn more about the occupation and apartheid of Palestine read [the] UN's report on Israeli settlements in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, as well as reports from Human Rights Watch and Amnesty International. Additionally, we suggest reading the BDS National Committee's call to boycott, divest from, and sanction Israel until it complies with international law." The statement was signed in support by: Students For Justice In Palestine, Muslim Student Association, Black Students Association, LGBT+ & Allies, South Asian Student Association, Latin American Student Association, Deib Committee, Reinventing the Cycle, Dr. Philip Metres, Professor of English and Director of the Peace, Justice And Human Rights Program. Link 1 (Accessed: 4/19/23)

Short Description (New): JVP published a letter in Swarthmore Voices titled "Jewish Voice for Peace Letter of Support for the Boycott Sabra Campaign" that demonized Israel and supported BDS [1].
Description: JVP published a letter in Swarthmore Voices titled "Jewish Voice for Peace Letter of Support for the Boycott Sabra Campaign" that demonized Israel and supported BDS [1]. The letter stated, "Jewish Voice for Peace (JVP) at Swarthmore College is in full support of Students for Justice in Palestine (SJP)’s campaign to end the sale of Sabra products on campus. As outlined by SJP, Sabra is owned by the Strauss group, a corporation that sends funds to the Golani Brigade, a particularly violent arm of the brutal Israeli military. We are appalled that our institution, by buying Sabra products, funds the Golani Brigade and thus funds violations of Palestinians’ human rights. As a Jewish organization committed to freedom, justice, and equality for all people, we are dedicated to ending our community’s complicity with the illegal Israeli occupation and to promoting the right of Palestinian refugees to return to their homes, as stipulated in UN Resolution 194... Alongside supporting the call to end the sale of Sabra products on campus, we wholeheartedly condemn the claims, coming from anonymous petitions and paid advertisements, that this campaign is antisemitic. These hasbara tactics are a clear attempt to intimidate activists into silence and to artificially influence the conversation on campus via digital astroturfing. There is nothing antisemitic about calling for our institution to stop funding extreme violence perpetrated by the state of Israel... As Jews, as Swarthmore students, and as a group of individuals who care about human rights, we proudly join the call to end the sale of Sabra products on campus and encourage all who care about human rights to sign the petition as well: tinyurl.com/sabrasjp." Link 1

Short Description (New): An SJP member posted a new pledge titled, "Black Bruins: Pledge No Free Propaganda Trips to Apartheid Israel" to the UCLA Academic Advancement Program's Facebook Page.
Description: An SJP member posted a new pledge titled, "Black Bruins: Pledge No Free Propaganda Trips to Apartheid Israel" to the UCLA Academic Advancement Program's Facebook Page. The pledge demonizes and delegitimizes Israel, stating, "We, as Black Bruins who believe in the movement for justice and equality for the Palestinian people, pledge not to take free propaganda trips to apartheid Israel through Hillel’s Fact Finders Journey program, the ADL’s (Anti-Defamation League) Campus Leader Mission initiative, nor through any other Israel lobby groups. We also pledge to support the call for the University of California to divestment from companies that aid in, and profit from, apartheid and racialized State violence committed against the Palestinian people at the hands of the racist apartheid state of Israel." The pledge then smears Hillel and ADL, stating, "Despite the Maccabee Task Force’s ties to White Supremacists, Hillel at UCLA willingly took sponsorship money from the Task Force to fund free propaganda trips to Israel in an attempt to coerce politically active students on campus against the Palestinian movement for equal rights...The Anti-Defamation League also sponsors free propaganda trips to Israel for student leaders through its Campus Leader Mission program. On top of its propaganda trips, the ADL has for years recruited police departments across the country to train in Israel. The tactics learned in Israel are militaristic, rooted in anti-Palestinian racism, and are used against Black communities in the US that already face a violent militaristic and racist police state...Black Bruins recognize how Black and Palestinian liberation is tied up in US empire. On top of racist policing collaborations, the US sends over $3 billion to Israel annually to fund its Jim Crow-like apparatus; meanwhile, it claims to have no money for Black people in Flint Michigan where the water continues to be contaminated. And just as the US supported the White supremacist government of apartheid South Africa by vetoing every UN Security Council Resolution, the US continues to support Israel in the international arena to push its imperialist policies that disproportionately impact the Black diaspora." Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): A wall and sidewalk near Baker University Center used for campus graffiti free speech was vandalized with 11 spray-painted swastikas, a depiction of Hitler, and a racial slur.
Description: A wall and sidewalk near Baker University Center used for campus graffiti free speech was vandalized with 11 spray-painted swastikas, a depiction of Hitler, and a racial slur. Image credit: Cleveland Jewish News. Link 1
University Response: Carly Leatherwood, senior director of communications services at OU, released a statement that read, "On April 27, Ohio University was made aware of offensive words and symbols that were spray painted on our Graffiti Wall. We condemn these words, symbols and their meanings, which are absolutely counter to our university’s core values of diversity and inclusion. As an institution of higher learning, we have a fundamental responsibility to promote inquiry and learning, and it is our hope that the Graffiti Wall will continue to teach our university community that words and images are powerful. We are extremely grateful to our partners at Hillel and other community members for their swift action to help replace the hateful words and imagery with sentiments of hope and kindness."

Description: The Associated Student Government passed a BDS resolution titled “Resolution Concerning the Response to the Israeli Occupation of Palestine” in a roll call vote of 20-2, where the Senate additionally passed an amendment accusing Israel of "genocide" and "Zionist settler colonialism" [1]. According to Daily Northwestern, the resolution has three points: "ASG’s majority support of Palestinian human rights, demands that the University issue a solidarity statement and calls for NU to divest from six companies that have aided Israel’s aggression toward Palestinians." During the meeting, Jewish students were subjected to denigration and bullying from other students in the chat, including one being told he has "worms for brains", and a response of "stop all lives mattering this" as well as "just say you don't see Palestinians as human if you are against this amendment" for pointing out that "disagreeing with this resolution does not mean you disagree with Palestinian liberation" and statements of "condemning Hamas right now is the same as condemning someone for scratching their rapist" , "true jihad" and "this is people's lives not your f*cking story" [2]. Link 1, Link 2 [Submittal]

Short Description (New): Jewish Law Students for a Free Palestine published [1] a letter [2] to "the NYU Law and Broader Community" that called to de-platform Zionists and for the Dean's office to withdraw their participation from the event "Antisemitism: An Assault on Human Rights and Our Shared Humanity".
Description: Jewish Law Students for a Free Palestine published [1] a letter [2] to "the NYU Law and Broader Community" that called to de-platform Zionists and for the Dean's office to withdraw their participation from the event "Antisemitism: An Assault on Human Rights and Our Shared Humanity". The letter stated, "We write to you as Jewish law students and anti-zionists, with concerns about the upcoming talk by Michal Cotler-Wunsh. Antisemitism is a dangerous and pervasive social force, and it should be taken seriously. However, antisemitism is distinct from anti-zionism. By platforming a speaker who vocally equates the two at an event about antisemitism, NYU perpetuates this false equivalence, weaponizing antisemitism to silence criticism of Israel. We write to express our support for the letter sent by Law Students for Justice in Palestine (LSJP) and echo their call for the Dean’s Office to withdraw their participation from this upcoming event. We encourage folks to sign on to this request, boycott the event, and join the LSJP led teach-in on 4/20 at 6pm in the Vanderbilt Courtyard. We also wish to uplift the Solidarity Bylaw passed by our peers, and ask for members of the student body and the administration writ large to make a commitment to stop platforming zionist speakers or promoting zionist events... Michal Colter-Wunsh conflates antisemitism and anti-zionism. Zionism is distinct; it is a settler-colonial ideology rooted in the ethnic cleansing of the indigenous Palestinian population. Support for an anti-imperial, anti-zionist analysis that centers the Palestinian people in their struggle for autonomy, human dignity, and self-determination is not, and cannot be, evidence of discrimination against Jewish people. Over 60 organizations, including Human Rights Watch, B’Tselem, the ACLU, and Defense for Children International, recently wrote a letter to the UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres opposing the IHRA definition of antisemitism that Cotler-Wunsh supports. In their letter, they note that the conflation of anti-zionism and antisemitism has been used to suppress criticism of Israel and advocacy for Palestinian rights, especially by Palestinian, Arab, and Muslim advocates for Palestinian self-determination... Once again, in the midst of violence against Palestinians at Al Aqsa Mosque and throughout Israel-Palestine, during the deadliest year on record for Palestinians since 2006, NYU uplifts narratives supportive of the Israeli state. The administration continues to center zionist voices on campus and silence support for Palestinian liberation. In the days after Passover, as we reflect on collective liberation and freedom, we want to emphasize that the organizations supporting this event do not speak for us. The Jewish Law Students Association does not speak for us, the Bronfman Center does not speak for us, Law Students Against Antisemitism does not speak for us, and the NYU Administration does not speak for us. We will continue to speak out against zionism in all its forms and support Palestinian people’s right to autonomy, human dignity, and self-determination." The letter was signed by 16 anti-Zionist students. Link 1 (Accessed: 4/24/23), Link 2 (Accessed: 4/24/23)

Short Description (New): SJP published a guest column titled "To hell with 'meaningful dialogue': Why we walked out on Naftali Bennett" in The Duke Chronicle, which demonized Israel and expressed, "Hosting Bennett on campus was a shameless attempt to normalize Israeli apartheid," "Duke University is complicit in apartheid," and "Hosting Zionist leaders is an act of normalization — one that we refuse to let go unprotested" [1].
Description: SJP published a guest column titled "To hell with 'meaningful dialogue': Why we walked out on Naftali Bennett" in The Duke Chronicle, which demonized Israel and expressed, "Hosting Bennett on campus was a shameless attempt to normalize Israeli apartheid," "Duke University is complicit in apartheid," and "Hosting Zionist leaders is an act of normalization — one that we refuse to let go unprotested" [1]. SJP stated,: "On March 22, we walked out of a Duke event featuring former Israeli Prime Minister Naftali Bennett. Hosting Bennett on campus was a shameless attempt to normalize Israeli apartheid... Silent participation condones our university’s complicity in Israeli apartheid. Walking out has long been a strategy for students to oppose institutional complicity and protest for social change... Since its establishment in 1948, the Israeli state has continually ethnically cleansed Palestinian inhabitants to further its settler colonial objectives. Zionism aims to establish a Jewish-majority state in Palestine. An ethnocratic state like Israel can only thrive with an infrastructure that continually removes non-Jewish inhabitants. Consequently, Israel has continued to expand settlements; as of 2021, 700,000 Israeli settlers had entered the West Bank and East Jerusalem. This practice, deemed illegal under international law, entails the violent expulsion of countless Palestinian families and their replacement by Israeli settlers... Naftali Bennett, self-described as 'more right-wing' than Benjamin Netanyahu, is but one actor in the larger Zionist settler colonial project... Duke University is complicit in apartheid. Hosting Zionist leaders is an act of normalization — one that we refuse to let go unprotested... Direct activism by Palestinians and their supporters via social media has arguably begun to shift public opinion in ways that could significantly alter the U.S.-Israeli relationship. Internationally, the Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions movement is forcing global actors to question their complicity in Israel's military aggression toward Palestinians. Our act of protest is justified. We aim to counter the widely propagandized Zionist narrative — a narrative that erases the Palestinian people and their struggle for liberation. We encourage our fellow students to step off the sidelines and join the movement for justice in Palestine." Link 1 (Accessed: 3/30/23)

Short Description (New): SJP released a statement titled "GW Students for Justice in Palestine Statement on Title VI Complaint Filed Against GW by Palestine Legal," which demonized Israel, denigrated Zionists as "racist" and "right-wing", claimed "GW has proven time and time again that it will continue to align itself with the Zionist lobby, choosing the interests of right-wing, pro-Israel organizations over the safety and protection of its Palestinian and Arab community members" and ended with statements intended to suppress Zionist expression and bully Zionist students, such as, "SJP stands firmly by our principles and continues to combat Zionism on campus and on ALL fronts" and "Long Live the Intifada" [1].
Description: SJP released a statement titled "GW Students for Justice in Palestine Statement on Title VI Complaint Filed Against GW by Palestine Legal," which demonized Israel, denigrated Zionists as "racist" and "right-wing", claimed "GW has proven time and time again that it will continue to align itself with the Zionist lobby, choosing the interests of right-wing, pro-Israel organizations over the safety and protection of its Palestinian and Arab community members" and ended with statements intended to suppress Zionist expression and bully Zionist students, such as, "SJP stands firmly by our principles and continues to combat Zionism on campus and on ALL fronts" and "Long Live the Intifada" [1]. The full statement read, "Yesterday, February 14th, Palestine Legal filed a Title VI complaint with the Department of Education against GWU detailing a climate of anti-Palestinian discrimination by university administration. The complaint details a long history of discriminatory, anti-Palestinian actions taken by University administration in an attempt to repress and silence Palestinian organizing. SJP stands in full support of this complaint, as this is a history that SJP has faced many times. Our organization, our members, and other members of the Palestinian and Arab community at GW have faced continued waves of discriminatory action taken by an administration that continues to wage attack after attack on Palestinian community members rather than protect us from doxxing and harassment and standing by our right to free political expression. The complaint details the forced cancellation of a Palestinian mental health support service organized by the Office of Advocacy and Support in the wake of the devastating ‘Israeli’ bombing of Gaza in 2021 that murdered over 230 Palestinians in cold blood. SJP stands in full support of the OAS staff that were threatened and intimidated by GW and Hillel administration, and the students stripped of a space to process the grief and anger of yet another wave of mass slaughter enacted on our people by the Zionist Entity. The complaint also details the campaign of racist defamation against acclaimed Dr. Lara Sheehi, spun by right-wing Zionists seeking to punish Dr. Sheehi, an Arab woman, for her consistent, unwavering support for Palestinian liberation. The complaint also details how Palestinian students, following a class in which other students berated Dr. Sheehi, while using racist, anti-Palestinian, and anti-Black language, were coerced into a ‘restorative circle’ where facilitators accused them of ‘causing harm’ to Jewish students for defending Dr. Sheehi. SJP stands in full support of Dr. Sheehi who is currently facing a farcical investigation by GW administration, and the Palestinian students who have faced hostile peers, racist remarks, and an administration who, rather than protecting those students, turned on them in retribution for voicing their concerns. The complaint also details the absurd, racist charges faced by our organization last semester and the blatantly racist police targeting and profiling of SJP's president and members in a series of kangaroo court hearings, in which we were cleared of ALL charges. The complaint also details the defamatory remarks made by Adena Kirstein, executive director of GW Hillel, who falsely accused our organization of antisemitism and falsely claimed that the SJP President was personally responsible for the very charges we were cleared of, in an email publicized to the entire GW Hillel listserv. GW has proven time and time again that it will continue to align itself with the Zionist lobby, choosing the interests of right-wing, pro-Israel organizations over the safety and protection of its Palestinian and Arab community members. As Palestinians, as Arabs, we know the ways in which our community is attacked because the liberation of our people is considered a political threat to the interests of powerful entities who maintain and profit from the status quo of imperialism, settler colonialism, and military occupation. SJP stands in full support of the complaint filed by Palestine Legal, and maintains unwavering dedication to ensuring that those who oppress and attack our people do not get to continue doing so without consequence. We echo the demands laid out by Palestine Legal that GW take measures to remedy the harm done to Palestinian students and faculty and to protect community members from future anti-Palestinian actions. SJP is monitoring this complaint very closely and will continue to organize around this campaign to end anti-Palestinian discrimination at GW and at universities across the world. Our people will NOT lie down in the face of discrimination or attack and our liberation will NOT be compromised. SJP stands firmly by our principles and continues to combat Zionism on campus and on ALL fronts. Protect Palestinian Students, Protect Palestinian Faculty, Long Live the Intifada. [Signed] GW Students for Justice in Palestine." Link 1

Short Description (New): The UC Palestine Collective, comprised of students, faculty and community members across the UC system, released a petition and statement that demonized Israel, denigrated UC Regent Richard Leib and demanded his "immediate resignation" because of his support for Israel, as expressed via "likes" on his personal Twitter/X account, which the collective called "homophobic" and "racist." [1-2]
Description: The UC Palestine Collective, comprised of students, faculty and community members across the UC system, released a petition and statement that demonized Israel, denigrated UC Regent Richard Leib and demanded his "immediate resignation" because of his support for Israel, as expressed via "likes" on his personal Twitter/X account, which the collective called "homophobic" and "racist." [1-2] The statement read, in part: "As a collective of students, faculty, and community members across the University of California (UC) System, we write to bring attention to the deeply disturbing, homophobic, and racist social media behavior of UC Regents Chair Richard Leib, who is arguably the highest-ranking individual in the entire UC System... Leib recently liked a post published on X (dated 11/05/2023) that equated a group of protestors holding a sign, 'Queers for Palestine,' with an image of cows bearing a sign that read 'Cows for McDonald’s.' We condemn in the strongest terms this shameless display of pinkwashing, which maliciously wields queer identities as a tool to justify Israel’s ongoing genocide and illegal occupation of Palestine... Leib has liked multiple posts claiming that Hamas uses hospitals as military headquarters... Leib's endorsement of misinformation justifies the continuation of these atrocities abroad... Leib has also liked several Tweets ridiculing the Boycott, Divestments, and Sanctions (BDS) Movement... Leib and UC President Michael Drake authored a public statement to all members of the UC community that perpetuated a dangerously one-sided, racist, and dehumanizing narrative against Palestinians [for their condemnation of the Hamas October 7th terror attack]... One year earlier, in October 2022, Leib also issued a public statement that denounced the efforts of law students at UC Berkeley to stand against the apartheid state of Israel, violating their constitutional rights to engage in principled activism for Palestinian liberation [their "stand" was adding a bylaw to expressly exclude Zionist speakers and supporters of Israel from being allowed to speak]... Leib has done irreparable harm to Palestinian, Arab, Muslim, and queer communities and their allies, and does not possess the credibility or integrity to continue serving in a leadership role within the UC System... We demand the immediate resignation of UC Regents Chair Richard Leib, his removal from all committees related to Palestinian students and anti-Israel activism on UC campuses, and a public condemnation of his racist and homophobic social media engagement." Link 1 (Accessed: 12/01/23), Link 2 (Accessed: 12/01/23)
Perpetrator(s): Student group(s), Individual student(s), Individual faculty member(s), Faculty group

Description: UMass Boston SJP organized an off-campus "March for Palestine" [1], where, according to an observer, Jewish organizations were denigrated and a Zionist attending the open event was singled out by a speaker, and then verbally and physically assaulted by the event's participants, who shoved him, poured water on him and spat on him to chants of "Zionists go home" [2]. JNS reported that the event included a March to the local offices of the Anti-Defamation League (ADL) and the Jewish Community Relations Council (JCRC). A speaker explained they were marching to these offices because "[ADL-funded] trips for police have resulted in an escalation of militarization and lethality of Massachusetts police [and the trips to Israel funded by the JCRC] poison our representatives [and constitute an] investment in white supremacy." The speaker demanded that "the ADL and JCRC set up a truth council and pay restitution to black and indigenous communities that they have harmed in Massachusetts." Another speaker, referred to as Chance, claimed the ADL, JCRC and "a bunch of other Zionist organizations [are] some of the most powerful organizations in Boston, represent[ing] some of the most influential sections of the ruling class in this country." Chance further called law-enforcement agencies "the scum of the earth" and labeled the ADL a "terrorist organization." A local educator, Nino Brown, called Israel a "cancer to the Middle East". Participants chanted, "Long live the intifada!" and "There is only one solution, intifada revolution!" while marching to the offices. When outside the ADL offices, a speaker asked, "Is [Name of Zionist] in the crowd?" and Nino Brown took over the microphone to shout, "[Name of Zionist is]over here is in the crowd. He’s a rabid Zionist with this group called CAMERA [crowd boos]. If you are a Zionist here in the crowd, go home." Brown then led the crowd in chants of "Zionists go home." The crowd then surrounded the attending Zionist, prevented his exit, and called him a "Nazi". He was then shoved by one member of the crowd, doused with water by another member of the crowd, called a "f*cking pig" and spat on by yet another of member of the crowd [3]. Link 1, Link 2, Link 3
Perpetrator(s): Student group(s), Off-campus individual(s), Individual student(s)

Short Description (New): Students affiliated with Jewish Voice for Peace and If Not Now published an "Open Letter from Jewish Wolverines" that denigrated Hillel and demonized Israel [1].
Description: Students affiliated with Jewish Voice for Peace and If Not Now published an "Open Letter from Jewish Wolverines" that denigrated Hillel and demonized Israel [1]. The statement read, "We are Jewish students at the University of Michigan, affiliated with Jewish Voice for Peace and If Not Now. We are writing this letter because Hillel leaders are claiming once again to represent all Jews on campus as they harass members of Central Student Government for their powerful statement of solidarity with Palestinian people. As Jewish Wolverines, we are proud to see our student government supporting Palestinian liberation. Their statement represents us. We join them in expressing solidarity with the Palestinian people experiencing the ongoing Nakba of ethnic cleansing, genocide and apartheid. Hillel student leaders in private correspondence have told CSG their statement is antisemitic. That is a lie. Jewish identity cannot be reduced to the actions of the Israeli government. In conflating Zionism with Judaism, and conflating any truth-speaking about Zionism as antisemitism, Hillel leaders and their supporters are doing a disservice to the broader Jewish community. In fact, our own history of displacement and state violence calls us to stand with the Palestinian people. This is part of a pattern where Hillel claims to represent Jewish students on campus, while targeting students showing solidarity with Palestine. However, Hillel cannot support the multitude of Jewish experiences on campus as an organization that is funded by right-wing anti-queer, racist, and Islamaphobic organizations. We encourage Jewish students who feel defensive about the statement to do some self reflection on why a statement in support of a marginalized group experiencing state violence makes them feel that way. We also invite Hillel to consider the impact of demanding Muslim members of CSG to attend an antagonistic event with 'the Jewish community' on the Holy Day of Eid, which is already being desecrated by violence against Muslim and non-Muslim Palestinians by Israeli forces. We are asking our community, Jewish and non-Jewish, to sign up to voice your support for CSG’s solidarity at the next CSG meeting." Link 1

Short Description (New): Columbia University Students for Justice in Palestine and Columbia/Barnard Jewish Voice for Peace created a petition entitled "New Dual Degree Program Puts Columbia’s Academic Integrity at Risk," which claimed that by implementing a dual program with Tel Aviv University, "Columbia will import the racist policies of the Israeli State onto our campus".
Description: Columbia University Students for Justice in Palestine and Columbia/Barnard Jewish Voice for Peace created a petition entitled "New Dual Degree Program Puts Columbia’s Academic Integrity at Risk," which claimed that by implementing a dual program with Tel Aviv University, "Columbia will import the racist policies of the Israeli State onto our campus". The document stated, "Columbia University recently premiered a new dual-degree program between the School of General Studies (GS) and Tel Aviv University (TAU)... The university must recognize and address the full ramifications of such a program, and the threat it poses to the fabric of our campus community... We hope that such knowledge will prompt students, faculty, and administrators to push for the suspension of this program until Israeli academic institutions end their complicity in ethno-nationalist practices and military occupation... Columbia will import the racist policies of the Israeli State onto our campus... Millions of Palestinian refugees who live in the above listed countries also face similar restrictions. If only a certain percentage of students are eligible for this program on account of these exclusionary policies, Columbia will be guilty of importing the racist policies of the Israeli State onto our campus... Columbia risks violating Title VI of the Higher Education Act by operating a program that, as a matter of conforming to official Israeli government policy, would require the university to discriminate against students on the basis of their race and/or national origin with regards to their access to education... Columbia risks abetting systemic anti-Palestinian racism and reproducing the racial inequalities of Israeli policies on our own campus... TAU is remarkable for its explicit institutional culpability in the design and execution of war crimes and active military occupation... Far from a neutral academic or research institution, TAU is essentially an outsourced wing of the Israeli military, and the statements by university administrators and senior faculty parse this as a source of institutional pride... So long as Israel’s 'security interests' are negatively correlated with the rights and welfare of Palestinians, this arithmetic ensures that TAU’s academic successes will continue to be linked to increasingly brutal, systematic, and 'high-tech' oppression in the service of a militarized apartheid state. To collaborate with TAU is to tie Columbia’s institutional credibility to a war crimes pipeline... As it stands now, the dual degree program risks excluding a substantial portion of prospective students and importing the racist and discriminatory policies of the Israeli State onto Columbia University’s campus." Link 1

Short Description (New): A spray-painted swastika was discovered in a room on the fourth floor of Meyerson Hall in the Stuart Weitzman School of Design [1].
Description: A spray-painted swastika was discovered in a room on the fourth floor of Meyerson Hall in the Stuart Weitzman School of Design [1]. Link 1 (Accessed: 9/26/23)
University Response: President Liz Magill and a number of administrators responded with a statement expressing, "As many of you are aware, Penn Police responded yesterday to reports of an individual shouting antisemitic obscenities and overturning furniture at Penn Hillel. The individual was determined to be in crisis and was quickly and safely removed and referred for medical evaluation. This troubling incident came in the wake of another upsetting occurrence at the Weitzman School of Design, where a group of students found a swastika painted on the wall of a spray booth, a small room that is used for painting projects, on the fourth floor of Meyerson Hall. Dean Frederick Steiner wrote to the Weitzman community immediately after learning of the incident... We unequivocally condemn such hateful acts. They are an assault on our values and mission as an institution and have no place at Penn. Sadly, incidents of hatred, including antisemitic rhetoric and acts that denigrate Jewish people, have become all too common. That these incidents happened on our campus, in our spaces, is deeply unsettling. We also acknowledge the timing of these incidents is particularly difficult given the controversial speakers who will be participating in the event on our campus over the coming days. It is our collective responsibility as a community to stand clearly and strongly against antisemitism."

Classification: SUPPRESSION OF SPEECH/MOVEMENT/ASSEMBLY, DEMONIZATION, CALLS FOR BDS
Short Description (New): The Critical Ethnic Studies program released a statement [1] encouraging people to read the "CRES Statement of Solidarity with the Palestinian People on Nakba Day" [2] and claimed that the statement "reflects the Critical Ethnic Studies Program at California College of the Arts" where the CRES statement demonized Israel and stated endorsement for the "Palestine and Praxis" statement [3] that includes a commitment to academic BDS, which, if put into effect, would restrict the educational opportunities and academic freedom of students and faculty who want to study about or in Israel.
Description: The Critical Ethnic Studies program released a statement [1] encouraging people to read the "CRES Statement of Solidarity with the Palestinian People on Nakba Day" [2] and claimed that the statement "reflects the Critical Ethnic Studies Program at California College of the Arts" where the CRES statement demonized Israel and stated endorsement for the "Palestine and Praxis" statement [3] that includes a commitment to academic BDS, which, if put into effect, would restrict the educational opportunities and academic freedom of students and faculty who want to study about or in Israel. The CRES statement read in part: "As a program committed to the study of colonialism, military occupation, and Indigenous resistance, Critical Race and Ethnic Studies stands in support of the Palestinian people as they live under multiple forms of violence imposed on them by Israel. As an academic program housed on Turtle Island, we oppose settler colonialism everywhere and we condemn the land expropriation and settler colonial violence Palestinians have experienced for more than seven decades; we also note that this violence is facilitated by unwavering U.S. financial, military, and political support, which we vehemently oppose... Ethnic cleansing continues apace across Palestine... Palestinians are under constant threat by armed Israeli mobs looking for Arabs to attack. As ethnic studies scholars and activists committed to anti-colonial, anti-racist, feminist, and queer organizing, situated particularly in California, we see the terrain of violence against Palestinians and censorship against Palestinians in the diaspora clearly. We see and condemn the censorship of Palestinian scholars, Palestinian studies, and Arab American Studies in our classrooms and in the U.S. academy more broadly. For this reason, we reject the language of clashes, conflict, and 'both sides.' As we oppose policing and prisons, Indigenous dispossession, and racialized violence, from Turtle Island to Palestine, we refuse to use objective language that would render us silent as Palestinians continue to experience an ongoing Nakba. We stand with the protesters across Palestine, and around the world, in solidarity with Palestinian freedom struggles in the face of ceaseless violence. We urge other departments and programs to join us in issuing statements, alongside other forms of collective action, including worldwide protests today, Nakba Day, May 15th, to condemn ongoing Israeli state violence and express solidarity with the Palestinian people. As ethnic studies scholars endorsing Scholars for Palestinian Freedom’s 'Palestine and Praxis: Open Letter and Call to Action,' we affirm that 'scholarship without action normalizes the status quo and reinforces Israel’s impunity.' And, as ethnic studies scholars in solidarity with the Palestinian Feminist Collective, we end with their affirmation of life and love in their 'Love Letter to our People Struggling in Palestine:' 'Your labor has taught us for generations: Palestine is a feminist issue. Love guides our methodology for liberation. We affirm life and implore feminists everywhere to speak up, organize, and join the struggle for Palestinian liberation. We call for an immediate halting of the theft of homes in Sheikh Jarrah, Silwan, and beyond, and an immediate halting to the airstrikes on Gaza.' In solidarity, Critical Race and Ethnic Studies Program." Link 1 (Accessed; 10/25/23), Link 2 (Accessed: 10/25/23), Link 3 (Accessed: 10/25/23)

Classification: SUPPRESSION OF SPEECH/MOVEMENT/ASSEMBLY, DEMONIZATION, CALLS FOR BDS, DENYING JEWS SELF-DETERMINATION
Short Description (New): Skidmore SJP organized [1] a letter [2] "to all the Skidmore professors who have previously pledged their support as 'allies" to BIPOC students" [3] that demonized Israel and encouraged faculty endorsement for the "Palestine and Praxis" statement [4] that includes a commitment to academic BDS, which, if put into effect, would restrict the educational opportunities and academic freedom of students and faculty who want to study about or in Israel.
Description: Skidmore SJP organized [1] a letter [2] "to all the Skidmore professors who have previously pledged their support as 'allies" to BIPOC students" [3] that demonized Israel and encouraged faculty endorsement for the "Palestine and Praxis" statement [4] that includes a commitment to academic BDS, which, if put into effect, would restrict the educational opportunities and academic freedom of students and faculty who want to study about or in Israel. The letter read, "We have gathered to pen a letter [5] to Marc Conner stating how crucial it is for Skidmore to participate in the international Boycott, Divestment, Sanctions movement as an act of solidarity with Palestinians resisting Israeli occupation in Occupied Palestine. You are receiving this email because you put yourself down on the list of professors who consider themselves to be allies and to whom marginalized students can reach out to for support. This is us reaching out for your support in our call for Skidmore to participate in BDS by removing products from our campus which contribute to the apartheid in Occupied Palestine, and therefore make all of us complicit in the murder of Palestinian peoples. As many of you know, white supremacy manifests at the institutional level in both blatant and insidious ways. We believe that making an active decision to ignore, or not be in solidarity with Palestinians is in direct contradiction to our goals as individuals committed to justice. If you have ever said that you are committed to dismantling white supremacy, to practicing anti-racist or decolonial pedagogy, to standing up for academic freedom, or to allying yourself with the most vulnerable, solidarity with Palestine is your responsibility... We have created a petition, and we urge you and your fellow colleagues to sign it in support of this call. Your support is crucial, and is one of the many action steps you can take to support Palestinian students on this campus, and Palestinians resisting occupation abroad. As someone who committed to supporting marginalized students on this campus, we see this as your duty. Be mindful that if you are the head of a department, a faculty member with tenure status, or a white faculty member, your positionality gives you more power and influence on this campus than faculty of color and non-tenured faculty; because of this, we are expecting you to step up in more ways than simply signing this letter. We are calling on faculty members to: 1. Immediately sign, boost, and send out this petition to Skidmore colleagues. 2. Email (as an individual, or as a department) Marc Conner voicing your support for BDS more broadly, and for Skidmore students’ call for Skidmore to align itself with the BDS movement more specifically. 3. Support student activists: follow @SkidSJP (Skidmore Students for Justice in Palestine) for further updates regarding pro-Palestine actions on campus. 4. Push your academic organizations and associations to align with the BDS movement. 5. Make a continued effort to educate yourself on the occupation of Palestine and the resistance of Palestinian people in a manner that centers the work of Palestinians. Then, commit to teaching this in your classroom in a way that centers Palestinian experiences (ie: invite Palestinian guest lecturers into your classroom, assign the works of Palestinian scholars and activists). 6. Sign this letter, which is an international call, by Palestine and Praxis, for scholars to align themselves with the academic Boycott, and to commit to 'highlighting Palestinian scholarship.'" Image credit: Screenshot. Link 1, Link 2, Link 3, Link 4, Link 5

Short Description (New): The student government body passed a BDS resolution in a 22-11-7 vote [1], where the resolution, according to Chancellor Jones, "force[d] students who oppose efforts to divest from Israel to also vote against support for the Black Lives Matter movement" [2] and during the vote itself, a Jewish ISG Senator was shut out of entering the speaking queue to speak by the ISG Chair, despite having followed proper procedure to do so [5].
Description: The student government body passed a BDS resolution in a 22-11-7 vote [1], where the resolution, according to Chancellor Jones, "force[d] students who oppose efforts to divest from Israel to also vote against support for the Black Lives Matter movement" [2] and during the vote itself, a Jewish ISG Senator was shut out of entering the speaking queue to speak by the ISG Chair, despite having followed proper procedure to do so [5]. This resolution's passage followed on five failed attempts in recent years, and, according to Hillel, "was an attempt to paint Israel and Jews as the obstacle to racial equity." [3]. According to JNS, the resolution "called on the university to divest from Northrop Grumman, Raytheon Company, Lockheed Martin, Caterpillar Inc. and Elbit Systems Ltd. for what the resolution alleges is partaking in human-rights violations in the Palestinian territories... The resolution also called for the university to divest from its own police department, as well as the Champaign and Urbana Police departments, over alleged abuses, and for the university to divest from companies involved in the prison system, U.S. immigration enforcement and fossil fuels." [4] According to a Title VI complaint filed by the Brandeis Center for Human Rights Under Law, "A Jewish and pro-Israel ISG Senator had prepared a statement to read during the debate, and followed the proper procedure to enter the 'virtual' speaking queue to speak. The ISG Chair, however, precluded her from speaking by closing debate." [5] Link 1, Link 2, Link 3, Link 4, Link 5
University Response: Chancellor Robert J. Jones, along with Vice Chancellor for Academic Affairs and Provost Andreas C. Cangellaris and other administrative leaders issued a statement that said, in part, "Illinois Student Government (ISG) is an independent organization that can pass non-binding resolutions on any topic it chooses. It is unfortunate that a resolution before the group tonight was designed to force students who oppose efforts to divest from Israel to also vote against support for the Black Lives Matter movement. We are committed to dialogue and to supporting students as they navigate challenging conversations, and we will continue to plan programming designed to build understanding of different perspectives on complex and divisive issues. This resolution includes several points on which we can agree, but a foundational value of this institution is inclusion, and this resolution includes language that we cannot and will not support. As one of the country’s top public universities, we find ourselves in the difficult position of defending speech and expression, so we can talk together about difficult circumstances and have uncomfortable conversations. We must always balance that with our need to create a community where it is safe to live, learn and work."

Description: UC Davis Assistant Professor of American Studies Jemma DeCristo publicly called to dox "zionist journalists" and claimed "they have houses with addresses, kids in school" and "they can fear their bosses, but they should fear us more" with the image of a knife, ax, and blood drops [1-4]. The full message read, "one group of ppl we have easy access to in the US is all these zionist journalists who spread propaganda & misinformation. they have houses w addresses, kids in school. they can fear their bosses, but they should fear us more [Image of knife, ax, and three blood drops]." Image credit: Twitter. Link 1 (Accessed: 10/18/23), Link 2 (Accessed: 10/18/23), Link 3 (Accessed: 10/18/23), Link 4 (Accessed: 10/23/23), Link 5 (Accessed: 10/23/23)
University Response: UC Davis Chancellor Gary May stated, "I absolutely condemn the posts attributed to a UC Davis faculty member that recently appeared on the social media platform X. I find the comments revolting in every way, and I disagree wholeheartedly with them. UC Davis rejects all forms of violence and discrimination, as they are antithetical to the values of our university. We strive to foster a climate of equity and justice built on mutual understanding and respect for all members of the community. When we receive a complaint that a faculty member has engaged in conduct that may violate the Faculty Code of Conduct, we review the matter in accordance with our established policies and procedures for handling faculty discipline. These processes are confidential personnel matters that we are not permitted to share with the public, but we can confirm that the provost will refer this matter to the appropriate campus departments that investigate harassment, discrimination, and faculty conduct, in consultation with legal counsel regarding First Amendment rights. Some have asked why this faculty member continues to be employed at UC Davis. The University of California system has specific procedures for the review of complaints of faculty misconduct consistent with universitywide policies and bylaws. The status of complaints lodged against faculty members are confidential personnel matters, so we are unable to publicly comment on the steps we are taking. The public expression of opinions, even those opinions considered controversial or abhorrent, enjoy a high level of protection under the First Amendment. We are carefully reviewing this matter to ensure our response is consistent with universitywide policy and state and federal constitutional protections."

Short Description (New): In response to a Jewish student publishing an op-ed in the Jewish Journal [1] detailing her experiences with antisemitism on campus and harassment she experienced because of her Zionism [2], a petition [2] was created to support Palestinians on campus that belittled her concerns as "weaponiz[ing]" antisemitism and demonized Israel.
Description: In response to a Jewish student publishing an op-ed in the Jewish Journal [1] detailing her experiences with antisemitism on campus and harassment she experienced because of her Zionism [2], a petition [2] was created to support Palestinians on campus that belittled her concerns as "weaponiz[ing]" antisemitism and demonized Israel. The petition was signed by 21 student groups, including SJP and the Jewish Law Students Association, 121 students, 19 professors and faculty members, as well as a number of outside groups and individuals. The petition read, in part, "Palestinian activism on campus has a history of being particularly marginalized and attacked by people who support Israel. A subset of Zionist activists choose to weaponize the genuine threats of anti-Semitism elsewhere in our society as a tactic to repress activism and harass and threaten Palestinian students and Muslim students more broadly. This takes many forms: calling the presence of Palestinian students anti-Jewish, falsely labeling Students for Justice in Palestine as anti-Jewish, and even assuming any critique of Israel is rooted in anti-Jewish sentiment rather than a critique of Israel as a nation-state. We have seen this happening on our campus this year and we reject this misguided invocation of anti-Semitism as a weapon against people fighting for their own survival and right to exist... This tactic of conflating a critique of Judaism with critiques of Israel as a nation-state is on the rise around the country... [President Trump's] Executive Order and events on campus coincide with the Trump administration’s unveiling of a disastrous scheme intended to formalize the ethnic cleansing and ongoing colonization of Palestinian land, known as the 'Deal of the Century.'” Link 1, Link 2, Link 3
Perpetrator(s): Off-campus group(s), Individual student(s), Student group(s), Individual faculty member(s)

Short Description (New): The commencement speaker at the 2023 graduating class, Fatima Mohammed, a member of CUNY Law’s chapter of SJP denigrated Zionists and demonized Israel in her commencement speech [1-4].
Description: The commencement speaker at the 2023 graduating class, Fatima Mohammed, a member of CUNY Law’s chapter of SJP denigrated Zionists and demonized Israel in her commencement speech [1-4]. According to CUNY 4 Palestine, she "railed against Israeli settler colonialism, capitalism, imperialism and the racist police/carceral state as well as uplifted resistance" [1-2], and, according to JCRC-NY, she "attacked 'Zionists' and accused Israel of indiscriminately gunning down worshipers, murdering young and old people, and encouraging lynch mobs [as well as] linked CUNY funding and Zionism, leaning into antisemitic tropes about Jewish money and dual loyalty" [3]. In a video of her speech, Mohammed is shown stating, in part, "In this moment of celebrating who we are I want to celebrate CUNY Law as being one of the few, if not the only, public law school defending the right of it's students to organize and speak out against Israeli settler-colonialism [cheers of audience]. That this is the law school that passed and endorsed BDS on a student and faculty level, recognizing that absent a critical imperialism settler-colonialism lens, our work and this school's mission statement is void of value. That as Israel continues to indiscriminately rain bullets and bombs on worshippers, murdering the old, the young... as it encourages lynch mobs to target Palestinian homes and businesses... as it imprisons children... as it continues it's project of settler-colonialism, expelling Palestinians from their homes, carrying the ongoing Nakba, that our silence is no longer acceptable. We are the student body and faculty that fought back when investor-focused admin attempted to cross the BDS picket line saying loud and clear that Palestine can no longer be the exception to our pursuit of justice, that our morality will not be purchased by investors... CUNY Central, an institution that continues to fail us, that continues to train and cooperate with the fascist NYPD, the military, that continues to train IDF soldiers to carry out that same violence globally, a larger institution committed to its donors not to its students. I am here to remind us all that our existence on its own today in this room is revolutionary... Let us remember that Gaza has been bombed just this week with the world watching. That daily, brown and black men are being murdered by the state at Rikers. That there are Palestinian political prisoners... in U.S. prisons... that yesterday marked one year since the murder of U.S. journalist Shireen Abu Akleh... So may the joy, excitement and rage that fills this auditorium... be the fuel for the fight against capitalism, racism, imperialism and Zionism around the world... [W]e will protect the fight that brings us all closer to the fall of all oppressive institution[s], a reality that is only myopic and unrealistic to the oppressors, but is the inevitable future for the oppressed, for the oppressed people everywhere, for greater empires of destruction have fallen before and so will these."[4].Link 1 (Accessed: 5/18/23), Link 2 (Accessed: 5/18/23), Link 3 (Accessed: 5/18/23), Link 4 (Accessed: 5/29/23 - Starting at 1:15:30)
University Response: The Board of Trustees and Chancellor of the City University of New York released a statement approximately two weeks after the speech stating that "the remarks by a student-selected speaker at the CUNY Law School graduation, unfortunately, fall into the category of hate speech as they were a public expression of hate toward people and communities based on their religion, race or political affiliation."

Short Description (New): SJP released a statement to the community that denigrated Zionists on campus, denied Jews self-determination as well as demonized and delegitimized Israel.
Description: SJP released a statement to the community that denigrated Zionists on campus, denied Jews self-determination as well as demonized and delegitimized Israel. The statement said, "Students for Justice in Palestine (SJP) is and will always be against Zionism...Zionism is the creation of a Jewish state on top of the pre-existing Palestinian state, and maintaining such a state. Zionism started in Europe and was a political ideology created by European Jews to 'come back' to land which they have no physical claim to for over 2,000 years. This ideology is no different from White Man’s Burden or Manifest Destiny, which are all the justifications of colonialism and imperialism...We will always stand against any Zionist group, and to eradicate this practice, just like any other form of racism, we will fight against it. If MSA held programs in favor of ISIS, we would be the first ones to protest them...Israel is no different from apartheid South Africa, and the world came together to condemn apartheid and denominations of racism, ethnic cleansing, colonialism, and imperialism. Thus, why should Stony Brook University allow Israeli programs to be under the guise of interfaith?...In response to the quote mentioned from the community pledge to accept and respect the identity of students, we ask the university: if there were Nazis, white nationalists, and KKK members on campus, would their identity have to be accepted and respected? Absolutely not. Then why would we respect the views of Zionists? Regarding our statement about wanting a proper Jewish organization, any proper organization regardless of religion, should not prescribe to any denomination of racism, colonialism, or imperialism, including Zionism... we want to send a message to Chaplain Sanaa Nadim. You have reached a heinous level of betrayal to the Palestinian people by working with and aiding Zionists on their endeavors. For 3 years we have been on this campus, you have...helped Hillel normalize their Zionist agenda...We will work tirelessly to ensure that Stony Brook University’s MSA has a chaplain that properly represents the Palestinian liberation movement....Now, to the student body, we implore you to rise up and take part in demonstrations with us, express your qualms for states that are killing our people, and always fight for the right of the indigenous against these oppressive regimes. Together, we can create a domino effect to ensure Zionism is an extinct ideology." Link 1

Short Description (New): The Daily Pennsylvanian published a letter addressed to President Magill, Provost Jackson, and Dean Fluharty from 36 professors which denigrated Jews who were concerned about the history of antisemitic rhetoric of speakers presenting at the Palestine Writes event, as well as denigrated President Magill for her statement of concern regarding these speakers [1].
Description: The Daily Pennsylvanian published a letter addressed to President Magill, Provost Jackson, and Dean Fluharty from 36 professors which denigrated Jews who were concerned about the history of antisemitic rhetoric of speakers presenting at the Palestine Writes event, as well as denigrated President Magill for her statement of concern regarding these speakers [1]. The letter stated, in part, "As professors of literature and the humanities and as educators across the disciplines, it is with deep concern that we read your statement on the Palestine Writes Literature Festival. The statement says that 'While the Festival will feature more than 100 speakers, many have raised deep concerns about several speakers who have a documented and troubling history of engaging in antisemitism by speaking and acting in ways that denigrate Jewish people. We unequivocally — and emphatically — condemn antisemitism as antithetical to our institutional values.' However, as you are well aware, in recent years, many critics of Israeli occupation, and the oppression of Palestinian people and Arabs within Israel by the Israeli state, have been branded as antisemitic by Zionist organizations and individuals. Your statement gives the impression that the entire festival could be seen as hosting views that you see as 'incompatible with our institutional values.' ... It is equally important for us as educators to declare our support for Palestinian artists and writers, making it clear that we condemn antisemitism as well as Islamophobia and the oppression of Palestinians. Given the rise of hate speech and hate crimes against Muslims, Jews, and other vulnerable minority groups, the statement as it stands would make Palestinian students and their allies on this campus feel that they do not have the support of the University. The University needs to recognize that there is a great deal of bigotry on the part of those who are hostile to Palestinian rights. Instead of valuing some lives over others, the University should make a clear distinction between the affirmation of Palestinian rights, culture, and literature, and the practice of antisemitism. We ask that as leaders of the Penn community, you immediately amend your statement so that it is clearly in support of a diversity of views and diversity of religious, racial, and cultural communities on campus. Literature teaches us to engage in critical thought, it teaches us empathy, and it also teaches us that complex issues cannot be reduced to simple wars of position. Palestinian literature, like any other, is deeply intertwined with the dreams and aspirations of the Palestinian people; it also expresses their agony and resistance to occupation. We ought to respect that, and welcome a discussion of Palestinian literature, just as we would a festival exploring literature from any other part of the world or any other group of people. Let us celebrate our diversity and also the work of literature and the humanities. The Palestine Writes Literature Festival should not be singled out for censure. Neither should it be denied the diversity of viewpoints that comprise the Palestinian condition. "[1]. Link 1 (Accessed: 9/19/23)

Description: A rash of antisemitic stickers and flyers swept through campus November 15 – November 18, 2015. On November 15, Central Florida Future newsstand near Tower IV was defaced with a sticker that showed the Star of David and the words “1%” and “Bankers” underneath, and a second sticker that had a swastika on a flag with a message calling for an Israel boycott. The same day, a residence assistant for on-campus housing reported antisemitic vandalism on her building’s bulletin board where one sticker had a swastika and the others contained the phrases “Muh Holocaust” and “Truth is Treason In the empire of lies.” Also that same day, an officer responded to a call about antisemitic stickers placed on a community mailbox on Libra Drive. Earlier that day, a sticker with a Star of David and the phrase “1 percenters” was found on a street sign near the intersection of East Plaza Drive and Gemini Boulevard. Link 1

Short Description (New): Professor David Letwin, an arts instructor in the Mason Gross School of the Arts, published a letter he sent to President Holloway that endorsed an academic boycott of Israel and utilized demonizing language of Israel [1], which was subsequently used in SJP's campaign to claim, "Rutgers professors, too, are calling for the administration to terminate its agreement with Tel Aviv University"[2].
Description: Professor David Letwin, an arts instructor in the Mason Gross School of the Arts, published a letter he sent to President Holloway that endorsed an academic boycott of Israel and utilized demonizing language of Israel [1], which was subsequently used in SJP's campaign to claim, "Rutgers professors, too, are calling for the administration to terminate its agreement with Tel Aviv University"[2]. Letwin's letter stated, "As a Rutgers University employee, a member of the Rutgers Adjunct Faculty Union (PTLFC-AAUP-AFT), a New Jersey resident, and a co-founder of Jews for Palestinian Right of Return, I am writing to voice my opposition to the recent Memorandum of Understanding you signed between Rutgers and Tel Aviv University (TAU) 'to enhance the existing partnership between the two universities.' In 2021, three prominent human rights organizations published reports exposing the Israeli regime’s systemic oppression of the Palestinian people. Human Rights Watch released 'A Threshold Crossed,' which found that Israel is 'committing the crimes against humanity of apartheid and persecution.' Jerusalem-based B’Tselem declared Israel a 'regime of Jewish supremacy from the Jordan River to the Mediterranean Sea' ('This is Apartheid')... These powerful indictments — often in language unprecedented for mainstream human rights NGOs — affirm what Palestinians themselves have been telling the world since the Israeli state was forcibly established on Palestinian land in 1948 through an ongoing campaign of mass ethnic cleansing (the Nakba). Indeed, Palestinian resistance to Israel only makes sense when viewed through the lens of this violent displacement and all that has followed from it. As the Palestinian-led campaign for Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions against Israel (BDS) explains, far from opposing this regime of legalized discrimination and oppression, Israeli universities — including public institutions like TAU — 'are involved in developing weapon systems and military doctrines deployed in Israel’s recent war crimes in Lebanon and Gaza, justifying the ongoing colonization of Palestinian land, rationalizing gradual ethnic cleansing of indigenous Palestinians, providing moral justification for extra-judicial killings, systematically discriminating against ‘non-Jewish’ students, and other implicit and explicit violations of human rights and international law.' For these reasons, BDS, which echoes the historic divestment campaign that helped topple apartheid South Africa, specifically calls for the boycott of Israeli academic institutions. Yet at the very time that awareness of systemic Israeli racism and settler-colonial violence is surging, and as the world mourns the recent passing of Archbishop Desmond Tutu, celebrated critic of both the South African and Israeli apartheid regimes, Rutgers is violating the Palestinian picket line by deepening ties with a complicit Israeli academic institution, all the while hiding behind whitewashing platitudes about 'growing partnerships,' 'educational and scientific exchanges,' and 'benefits to local economies.' You, personally, have spoken of building 'beloved community.' But I ask: In what vision of beloved community is there room for growing partnerships with a state where, in the words of Israeli scholar Ilan Pappe, 'the value of ethnic superiority and supremacy overrides any other human and civil value'? And can you explain how collaborating with a university guilty of enabling war crimes conforms to the vision you espouse for the university over which you preside? 'Inspired by the US civil rights and South African anti-apartheid movements,' writes BDS cofounder Omar Barghouti, 'BDS calls for ending Israel’s 1967 military occupation, full equality for Palestinian citizens of Israel, and the UN-stipulated right of Palestinian refugees to return to the homeland they were uprooted from. BDS categorically opposes all forms of racism, including anti-Semitism.' This is a call for justice that Rutgers — and anyone committed to beloved community — should get behind." Link 1, Link 2

Short Description (New): During the event “Gaza Under Siege,” sponsored by numerous departments and Yalies4Palestine, Jewish students who had been handing out flyers, which students tore up and threw back at the Jewish students exclaiming "Don't take the paper," were denied entry to the event for not pre-registering, even though other students who hadn't pre-registered were invited in. sitting outside the closed door of the event hall, the Jewish students listened in and heard speakers make the following remarks: “violent resistance movements often emerge in colonized spaces;” "[Israel aims to] inflict as much harm, damage, and death as possible;” and “No matter what the solution is—a two-state solution or a one-state solution—the Israeli state cannot remain the state of the Jewish people” [1-3].
Description: During the event “Gaza Under Siege,” sponsored by numerous departments and Yalies4Palestine, Jewish students who had been handing out flyers, which students tore up and threw back at the Jewish students exclaiming "Don't take the paper," were denied entry to the event for not pre-registering, even though other students who hadn't pre-registered were invited in. sitting outside the closed door of the event hall, the Jewish students listened in and heard speakers make the following remarks: “violent resistance movements often emerge in colonized spaces;” "[Israel aims to] inflict as much harm, damage, and death as possible;” and “No matter what the solution is—a two-state solution or a one-state solution—the Israeli state cannot remain the state of the Jewish people” [1-3]. Speakers of the event were Nadia Abu El-Haj (Barnard College), Amahl Bishara (Tufts University), Diala Shamas (Center for Constitutional Rights) with moderators Lisa Lowe (Yale University) and Erica Edwards (Yale University). The event was promoted by the head of Jonathan Edwards College in a weekly email. The event was co-sponsored by: Department of American Studies, Department of Anthropology, Program on Ethnicity, Race & Migration, Department of Religious Studies, Women, Gender, & Sexuality Studies, Center For Middle East Studies, Yale Ethnography Hub, The Edward J. And Dorothy Clarke Kempf Memorial Fund, Yalies4palestine and Black Feminist. Image credit: Event advertisement / Yale Ethnography Hub. Link 1 (Accessed: 11/8/23), Link 2 (Accessed: 11/8/23), Link 3 (Accessed: 11/8/23)
Perpetrator(s): Academic department(s), Individual student(s), Individual faculty member(s), Off-campus individual(s), Student group(s), Individual administrator(s) or staff member(s)
Sponsors: Department of American Studies, Department of Anthropology, Program on Ethnicity, Race & Migration, Department of Religious Studies, Women, Gender, & Sexuality Studies, Center For Middle East Studies, Yale Ethnography Hub, The Edward J. And Dorothy Clarke Kempf Memorial Fund, Yalies4palestine and Black Feminist.
University Response: The University released a statement that did not address the Jewish student's concerns and denied that the student was not allowed to attend the event, even though the Jewish student affirmed entry was denied even though there were empty seats. The University read, "One of several educational events on campus about the Hamas-Israel war took place on Monday, Nov. 6. The faculty who organized the event invited outside speakers, including tenured faculty members from other universities, as they are allowed to do under the university’s policies on academic freedom and free expression. Students and other community members of all backgrounds had an opportunity to attend the event, engage intellectually and respectfully, and ask questions and participate in the discussion. Other events, representing a diversity of views and scholarly perspectives, have taken place on Yale’s campus since the Oct. 7 Hamas terrorist attack, including events at the MacMillan Center for International and Area Studies, Yale Law School, the Yale School of Management, and the Yale Jackson School of Global Affairs. There have also been numerous gatherings to support Jewish, Israeli, Muslim, and Palestinian students, faculty, staff, and others with ties to the region. There was strong interest in the panel held on Nov. 6, and the room reached capacity before it began. A few students were not aware that organizers had required pre-registration, and even some students who had pre-registered were unable to enter due to space constraints. As a result, a small number of people listened from the hallway; because the speakers wore microphones, the discussion was audible outside the room. The event began with a reiteration of Yale’s freedom of expression policies and a request that no filming or photography take place. Administrators were present to ensure that the event proceeded in a way consistent with the university’s policies which allowed for it to take place without disruption. Opinions and positions from people of all backgrounds were expressed respectfully during the program."
