Classification: SUPPRESSION OF SPEECH/MOVEMENT/ASSEMBLY, DEMONIZATION, CALLS FOR BDS
Short Description (New): SJP at Moraine Valley Community College signed [1] SJP at UIC's "Letter of Solidarity" [2] for anti-Zionist groups to demand their universities suspend Israel abroad programs, where SJP at UIC's letter included: "SJP at UIC is undoubtedly aware that the issue of schools hosting study abroad and/or birthright trips to israel is not just a local concern, but something that almost every SJP chapter has to fight against;" "Study abroad trips to israel are inherently problematic since they promote tourism to a settler-colonial, apartheid state;" "Every SJP should uphold the standard of demanding their university to boycott study abroad trips to israel, as it is a huge step in having institutions participate in BDS (Boycott, Divest, and Sanctions) against israeli institutions and propaganda;" and stated that if people want to see "an end to Zionism on college campuses, sign this letter".
Description: SJP at Moraine Valley Community College signed [1] SJP at UIC's "Letter of Solidarity" [2] for anti-Zionist groups to demand their universities suspend Israel abroad programs, where SJP at UIC's letter included: "SJP at UIC is undoubtedly aware that the issue of schools hosting study abroad and/or birthright trips to israel is not just a local concern, but something that almost every SJP chapter has to fight against;" "Study abroad trips to israel are inherently problematic since they promote tourism to a settler-colonial, apartheid state;" "Every SJP should uphold the standard of demanding their university to boycott study abroad trips to israel, as it is a huge step in having institutions participate in BDS (Boycott, Divest, and Sanctions) against israeli institutions and propaganda;" and stated that if people want to see "an end to Zionism on college campuses, sign this letter". A number of SJP chapters across the United States signed the letter. The full letter stated, "Students for Justice in Palestine at UIC has received a lot of media attention in the past week, and we are eternally grateful for everyone showing support. By now, we hope you understand our grievances and why we have chosen to file a federal civil rights complaint against UIC with the help of Palestine Legal. As many of you are aware, on January 23rd, students at the University of Illinois at Chicago were discriminated against on the basis of their Arab-sounding names and their affiliations with Palestine. They were barred from an informational Zoom session regarding a study abroad trip to israel, which was advertised as open to all UIC students. When the students changed their display names to White-sounding names, they were instantly let in but were later silenced when they began to ask questions about how the facilitators of the trip could guarantee their safety as Palestinian students and allies if they were to go on this trip. After that incident, Palestinian students and allies at UIC were threatened with legal action by UIC faculty, and targeted with violent action by the UICPD; in which an incident occurred on February 13th, where a Muslim UIC student, who wears the hijab, was grabbed by a UIC police officer, twice, over flyering on campus. SJP at UIC is undoubtedly aware that the issue of schools hosting study abroad and/or birthright trips to israel is not just a local concern, but something that almost every SJP chapter has to fight against. Study abroad trips to israel are inherently problematic since they promote tourism to a settler-colonial, apartheid state and blatantly ignore the ongoing occupation of Palestine. Not only does it economically benefit the state of israel, but it also promotes a sense of ignorance of the systemic suffering that Palestinians go through every single day. Because of this, we would like to mobilize SJPs all across the region to sign this letter of support with the goal of making this into a national campaign. If we band together as hundreds of SJPs across North America and take a stance on politically gray and unethical study-abroad trips, tangible change can happen. Every SJP should uphold the standard of demanding their university to boycott study abroad trips to israel, as it is a huge step in having institutions participate in BDS (Boycott, Divest, and Sanctions) against israeli institutions and propaganda. These are the demands we expect of UIC and every other university that hosts trips to the apartheid state of israel: 1. Universities should uphold the standard of not hosting trips to apartheid states and engaging in political and religious tourism that reinforces israeli propaganda. 2. Universities should hold open discussions with their Palestinian students and allies, and provide them a chance for dialogue in order to showcase their concerns/suggestions. 3. Universities should treat Palestinian students as equal to every other student demographic, and offer them equal access to resources and experiences. Hosting trips to israel fundamentally goes against this demand. If you stand in solidarity with SJP at UIC and would like to convey to the university the power there is in numbers, please sign this letter. You can be an individual, a faculty member, or an organization; if you want to see an end to anti-Palestinian discrimination, and an end to Zionism on college campuses, sign this letter. It is your duty as a human being to stand up against injustice and systems that perpetuate occupation, apartheid, and genocide. If you are an SJP chapter and would like to join SJP at UIC in their fight, please fill out the respective areas in the form. Hopefully, together we can create a widespread movement boycotting israeli study-abroad trips and bolstering the voice of the Palestinian revolutionary student movement in North America." Link 1 (Accessed: 10/6/23), Link 2 (Accessed: 9/28/23)

Short Description (New): SJP released a statement that denigrated Hillel [1].
Description: SJP released a statement that denigrated Hillel [1]. The statement read, "[W]e cannot stand idly by as we see UConn students and subsequently millions of individuals around the world slandered, unfairly labeled, and reduced to stereotypes-Muslims, Palestinians and allies alike... We continue to condemn UConn Hillel's damaging actions that have explicitly derailed said goals. Their actions transparently demonized the Palestinian struggle for justice and directly conflated the religion of Islam with terrorism. Students representing Hillel came to the protest with the sole intention of disrupting the demonstration by arguing with attendees of the protest and pushing rhetoric that vilifies millions of individuals. They created a divide and disrupted a space that was intended to amplify Palestinian voices as well as illuminate the human rights abuses and occupation of our people. Their actions in no way work to bring unity amongst students and are counterproductive to their stated goal of 'fostering meaningful and productive conversations among students' (UConn Hillel). A religious organization should work to fight against the oppression that all religions undoubtedly face. Yet, it is the content and rhetoric of which they put forth that is the true issue at hand. Hillel went on to state that Hamas is not representative of all Muslims, Palestinians or students supporting Palestine, yet brought pamphlets that blatantly conflated Hamas with all three. By passing out incredibly Islamophobic pamphlets to the student body, Hillel twisted our narrative and diverted attention away from Palestinian students cries for justice... Hamas is not representative of the views of the Palestinian people, and most definitely not representative of Islam's views. Hillel continues to hide their Islamophobic intentions behind a false promise of peace and friendship and we will absolutely not stand for this. The actions of Hillel unequivocally violate the comfort, safety and well-being of thousands of individuals on UConn's campus, and for there to be no consequences for the offending party is a blatant breach of the UConn student code and the UConn Student Bill of Rights. Our previous demands still stand. We demand that UConn, an administration that prides themselves on ethics and inclusion, holds Hillel and all students involved accountable for their actions, issue a public statement condemning these Islamophobic and discriminatory acts, and supports the safety and wellbeing of all oppressed parties. We also demand a thorough apology from the perpetrators, the Hillel students and staff involved, for their damaging actions. This is our final statement on the matter." Link 1

Description: Chalk writing on SFSU's campus targeted Zionists for exclusion, stating, "Zionists Not Welcome" and demonized Zionism stating, "Zionism = Racism." The statements appeared on campus in the main quad in reaction to President Wong’s campus-wide email apologizing to the Jewish community for his past expressions that had excluded Zionists and reiterating, "Let me be clear: Zionists are welcome on our campus.” The same day, also in response to Wong's email, GUPS issued a statement to the campus community claiming, "Zionism is the violent ideology responsible for the genocide and displacement of indigenous Palestinians and the destruction of Palestinian land. In 1948, Zionist militias destroyed over 500 Palestinian villages and exiled over 750,000 Palestinians from their homeland in order to establish the Zionist settler colonial State of Israel. Zionism is responsible for creating the largest refugee population in the world who have yet to return to Palestine." This was followed the next day by a Facebook post to GUPS supporters claiming, "Wong does not have the right to speak on behalf of an entire community while welcoming racist and colonial ideology on a college campus that is meant to educate students. Zionism is not welcome at SFSU or in any space where justice, freedom, and liberation are our goals. We stand united on the front lines of achieving liberation and justice in and for Palestine, academic freedom, and a campus free of hate, Zionism, and White supremacy." Image source credit: SF Hillel. Link 1, Link 2, Link 3

Short Description (New): SJP held a BDS event titled "The Fight for Freedom isn't Free".
Description: SJP held a BDS event titled "The Fight for Freedom isn't Free". The advertisement for the event stated, "In 2005, Palestinian Civil Society called for Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions against Israel until it complies with international law and universal principles of human rights. Since then a plethora of groups have responded to the call, such as student organizations, academics, activist, and civil rights leaders (the list goes on). In the light of anti-Palestine rhetoric, many states have passed anti-BDS bills that make it harder to participate in the boycott against Israel for human rights. Texas is one of the 26 states to pass an Anti-BDS bill, and we have seen the effects of this bill on campus. Come to our GBM to learn how our fight for freedom isn’t free." Link 1

Short Description (New): An open letter in the campus paper called on the board of Trustees to open up its investment portfolio in connection with a past BDS vote.
Description: An open letter in the campus paper called on the board of Trustees to open up its investment portfolio in connection with a past BDS vote. The open letter stated, " In 2014, the DePaul University administration was petitioned by pro-Palestine students to divest from pro-Israel investments, amidst opposition from pro-Israel lobbyists on campus. Even though the vote was successful in pulling funds from these causes, the board of trustees still maintains the confidentiality of their investment portfolio. Though this is within the university’s legal rights, a university that claims to be morally driven should – on principle – make their morally consequential behavior public to be held accountable for said behavior." Link 1

Short Description (New): After a discussion among themselves about BDS, the Student Government Association (SGA) issued a letter to the student body that rejected BDS-legislation requests made by protesters at a prior SGA meeting and addressed the protesters' concerns regarding two members of the SGA taking a trip to Israel.
Description: After a discussion among themselves about BDS, the Student Government Association (SGA) issued a letter to the student body that rejected BDS-legislation requests made by protesters at a prior SGA meeting and addressed the protesters' concerns regarding two members of the SGA taking a trip to Israel.The BDS call targeted the Georgia International Law Enforcement Exchange at GSU and the student protesters had also called for the resignation and an apology from SGA members for attending a free trip to Israel over winter break sponsored by GSU Hillel. The letter stressed, "As a state-funded school, the decision in regard to actionable BDS legislation either supporting or disagreeing will be determined by the Board of Regents and our state legislators." It continued by stating that the two SGA members who attended the Israel trip did so "as individual students in order to gain more knowledge and an educational perspective... During this trip, both [the SGA members] remained nonpartisan… [and did not] promote any political agenda to members of our Senate or any students prior to or following the trip... this trip provided [the SGA members] with personal insight into how truly diverse our University and the world is and provided new insight into how other groups live.” Link 1, Link 2, Link 3

Description: SJP announced more signatories to the groups' BDS Resolution, including the NYU Silver Undergraduate Student Government Association, making it the first school council to endorse the BDS resolution, out of a total of 59 campus groups. Additional new endorsers include Biracial and Multicultural Students Association, NYU-Wagner's Black Student Association, Justice Initiative at Global Public Health NYU, Map Club at NYU, and NYU NwB- Nutrition Without Borders. SJP claimed that with these new signers "an entire school" is "in support of this resolution!" Link 1

Short Description (New): The student government discussed and voted on a divestment resolution.
Description: The student government discussed and voted on a divestment resolution. The resolution failed with a 1-21 vote with three abstentions. Legislators debated on whether to overturn the decision, and the body failed to overturn the report in a 23-13 vote with one abstention. Twenty-nine campus groups, including the Behavioral and Social Sciences College, ProtectUMD and the University Senate had signed a petition of SJP's to support divestment alongside 200 individual signatures. More than 1,000 undergraduate students signed a petition against BDS, organized by Terps for Israel, J-Street UMD and Mishelanu. Link 1

Short Description (New): In a student-wide vote, students voted to endorse two anti-Israel resolutions, the first to condemn the presence of Caterpillar equipment on campus and the second to de-shelve Sabra hummus.
Description: In a student-wide vote, students voted to endorse two anti-Israel resolutions, the first to condemn the presence of Caterpillar equipment on campus and the second to de-shelve Sabra hummus. 67.4% of participating students voted for the Caterpillar resolution while 73.9% voted for de-shelving Sabra. Link 1

Short Description (New): An article in The Phoenix, the student-run newspaper, covered the formation of a new campus JVP chapter.
Description: An article in The Phoenix, the student-run newspaper, covered the formation of a new campus JVP chapter. The article quoted the two campus JVP leader's expressing support for BDS. The first leader was quoted as remarking, "Our goal is to create a space for Jewish students on campus to fight against the human rights abuses Israel is currently perpetuating and support BDS as a viable means to do so... We... recognize our positionality in all of this as one of incredible privilege and power. The fact is that when Jews speak out against Israel, people tend to listen, and we plan on using our platform to promote justice and equality for all people living in Israel/Palestine, and to support SJP’s BDS campaign in any way that we can." Another JVP leader stated, "JVP really fuses Jewish ritual with political activism, and uses the organization’s unique positionality as a Jewish leftist organization to support the BDS and other movements." Link 1

Short Description (New): A BDS resolution titled "CUNY Investment Policy" was tabled in the University Student Senate (USS) after strong criticism from the Jewish community about a lack of transparency.
Description: A BDS resolution titled "CUNY Investment Policy" was tabled in the University Student Senate (USS) after strong criticism from the Jewish community about a lack of transparency. The vote was tabled until April 24, when an updated version of the statement that recommends transparency will be available. The Jewish community was not informed of the BDS resolution until Shabbat, the day before the vote, and only then because it was leaked to a Jewish individual. According to USS's website, "The USS delegates are responsible for the representative governance of the 500,000 students of the twenty-five CUNY campuses." Link 2

Short Description (New): A petition entitled "USF Divest From Fossil Fuels, Private Prison and Companies Complicit in Human Rights Violations," that urges the USF Foundation to divest from "companies complicit in human rights violations," among other issues," was being pushed for placement as a referendum on the Student Government (SG) election ballot.
Description: A petition entitled "USF Divest From Fossil Fuels, Private Prison and Companies Complicit in Human Rights Violations," that urges the USF Foundation to divest from "companies complicit in human rights violations," among other issues," was being pushed for placement as a referendum on the Student Government (SG) election ballot. The petition includes companies investing "in human rights violations in Palestine and Yemen" but doesn't name specific companies. Last spring, the SG Supreme Court ruled that an SG Senate resolution specifically discussing divestment from companies investing in Israel was unconstitutional and not within the Senate's jurisdiction.A similar referendum made it to the ballot in 2013, which only discussed divestment from companies that were allegedly complicit in human rights violations. Legal counsel at that time had advised that the referendum was inconsistent with Florida Statutes and USF policies.This appears to be an attempt to skirt around these rulings. Link 1
